segments.s220-g385: دلِ مردِ بددل is a genitive/attributive group: دل (heart) + مرد (man) + بددل (bad‑hearted) — literally 'the heart of the bad‑hearted man.' گریزان ز تن is a present‑participle/participial adjective (گریزان = 'fleeing, escaping') with the preposition ز + تن marking what it flees from ('from the body'). Classical Persian often omits the ezāfe in writing, but the relationships are the same: 'the heart ... fleeing from the body.'
segments.s220-g386: دلیران is a plural noun 'the brave/valiant men.' ز خفتان بریده is a participial phrase: بریده (lit. 'cut off') used adjectivally with ز + خفتان ('from sleep') to mean 'deprived/roused from sleep' or 'torn from slumber.' کفن stands as a verbless nominal predicate (copula omitted, typical in poetry) indicating state — '(they were) in shrouds' or 'lay in their shrouds.'
segments.s220-g387: برفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they departed / they went away.' ازان جای is a prepositional phrase 'from that place' (ازان = 'from that', جای = 'place'). شیران نر is a descriptive noun phrase: شیران (here figuratively 'brave men' or 'lions') modified by نر ('male/person'), i.e. 'the male lions' or 'the valiant men.' Word order is typical of Persian: verb first, then source phrase, then subject phrase as a descriptive label.
segments.s220-g388: عقاب دلاور is a noun (عقاب = 'eagle') followed by the epithet دلاور ('valiant'). برآورد is used here as a past tense verb '(it) raised / lifted' and takes پر ('wing/feather') as its object, so the clause means 'the valiant eagle raised its wing.' Note: برآورد in this poetic use is an older/poetic form of برآوردن 'to raise' rather than the sense 'to estimate' found in some lexicons.
segments.s220-g389: نماند ایچ is a negative verb construction: نماند (past 'remained / was left') with ایچ (archaic 'nothing' = هیچ), yielding 'nothing remained.' با روی خورشید رنگ is an attributive phrase 'with the face/color of the sun' (با = 'with', روی = 'face/surface', خورشید = 'sun', رنگ = 'color/appearance') that describes what did not remain — a poetic inversion common in classical Persian.
segments.s220-g390: بجوش آمده is a compound describing a state/result: بجوش (root meaning 'to seethe/boil up') with آمده as an auxiliary/participial element meaning 'has come' → 'has boiled up' or 'seethed.' خاک is the subject 'earth/dust.' بر کوه و سنگ ('upon mountain and stone') is a locative phrase (بر = 'on/upon', کوه = 'mountain', و = 'and', سنگ = 'rock/stone'). The overall idea: 'the earth boiled up dust upon the mountains and rocks.'
segments.s220-g391: بلشکر here is a title 'the commander (of the army)' and functions as the topical/agent noun. چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said') and is followed by the name کاموس گرد (a compound proper name, here written as two words) in apposition: 'thus said the commander Kamus‑gerd.' Classical Persian often places the verb between modifier and the speaker's name in reported speech.
segments.s220-g392: که‌گر is a conditional/complementizer 'if' (classical form of که گر). آسمان را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker ('the sky/heavens' as definite object). بباید is a modal auxiliary 'it is necessary / one must' and سپرد is the bare infinitive/past stem of سپردن 'to hand over/entrust.' Together (که‌گر آسمان را بباید سپرد) expresses a conditional idea: 'if (it is) necessary to hand over the heavens.'
segments.s220-g209: ازان = archaic compound of از + آن (“from that”); it begins a prepositional phrase. چادر قیر is a noun + modifier construction meaning “tarred veil/cloak” (modifier follows or accompanies the noun in classical usage). بیرون کشید is a past-tense verb phrase (کشید = ‘pulled/drew’) with بیرون as adverbial=“out/aside.” The subject is omitted (third person); Persian often drops the pronoun when context supplies it.
segments.s220-g210: بدندان is an instrumental/adverbial phrase “with the teeth.” لب ماه is a compound description where لب (lip) is qualified by ماه (literally “moon,” used as epithet = ‘moon‑like/beautiful’). در خون کشید uses در + خون + کشید: here کشید (same root as earlier) means ‘drew’/‘caused to come’ and with در خون conveys the idiom ‘drew blood’ (i.e. bit hard enough to make blood). The verb is past and the subject remains the same omitted third person.
segments.s220-g211: تبیره برآمد: تبیره is a noun here (‘a sound/clamor’ in context; the dictionary senses given don’t match the poetic use), and برآمد is the past tense verb ‘arose/issued forth.’ ز هر دو سرای is a prepositional phrase ‘from both houses/dwellings’ (ز = ‘from’, هر دو = ‘both’, سرای = dwelling/house). Word order: subject (noun) + verb برآمد, then origin phrase.
segments.s220-g212: برفتند is past plural ‘they departed/advanced’; the plural subject is expressed immediately after (گردان لشکر = ‘the troop/ranks of the army’). ز جای is the adverbial ‘from (their) place/position’. Note the verb appears before the subject here — a permissible word order in older/narrative Persian to put the action up front.
segments.s220-g213: سپهدار هومان is a title + proper name (‘Commander Homan’) functioning as the subject. به پیش سپاه is a locative/directional phrase: به پیش = ‘to/in front of’, سپاه = ‘the army’. The preposition + noun state his position relative to the army.
segments.s220-g214: بیامد is past ‘he came’ (bi‑ prefix is archaic/poetic). همی کرد ... is an iterative/periphrastic construction: همی = ‘continually/kept’, کرد = past of کردن used here with نگاه (object) to mean ‘kept doing [the looking].’ هر سو = ‘every side/direction,’ نگاه = ‘gaze.’ So the clause literally: ‘he came; continually he did gaze every way’ = ‘he kept looking in every direction.’
segments.s220-g215: The clause begins with که introducing reported content (‘that’). ایرانیان را uses را as a topic/emphasis marker attached to ‘the Iranians.’ The second که + یار آمدست forms the reported clause ‘that a friend/ally has come’ (آمدست is an archaic perfect/past‑perfectic form = ‘has come’). Note the double که which is typical for linking explanatory/reporting clauses.
segments.s220-g216: Another clause introduced by که: ‘that خرگاه و خیمه بکار آمدست.’ خرگاه و خیمه is a coordinated nominal phrase ‘stalls/sheds and tents.’ بکار آمدست is a fixed expression meaning ‘has come into use / has been set up / is useful now’ (بکار = ‘for use’, آمدست = ‘has come’). The verb is again the archaic perfect; the coordination with و joins the two items.
segments.s220-g353: زبانی دگرگون is a noun phrase: زبانی (a tongue/language) is qualified by the adjective دگرگون (changed/different). بهر here is the preposition 'for/to/toward' and گوشه‌ای means 'every corner/quarter' (the indefinite -ی gives a distributive sense). Word order is typical Persian: modifier after the head noun; no ezafe is written in this poetic style.
segments.s220-g354: This is a coordinated nominal phrase listing regalia: درفش (banner/standard) modified by نوآیین ('newly fashioned' or 'of new ceremony') and coordinated with نو توشه‌ای ('and fresh provisions'). The و simply links the two coordinated items; نو is an adjective placed before توشه (poetic word-order variation).
segments.s220-g355: The clause begins with ز (poetic 'from/of' or 'there were'), introducing a list: پیلان (plural of پیل, 'elephants'), آرایش ('adornments/finery') and تخت عاج (a compound 'throne of ivory' with عاج postposed to تخت). The conjunction و links the list items; this is a paratactic enumeration typical of epic diction.
segments.s220-g356: همان here means 'the same' or 'likewise', introducing a parallel list: یاره (here 'attendants/followers'), افسر (in this context 'helmet' rather than officer), طوق ('necklace/collar') and تاج ('crown'). The repeated و simply separates the items in the enumeration.
segments.s220-g357: This is a simple verbless clause with بود (past of 'to be') serving as the verb: جهان (the world) بود یکسر (was entirely) چو (as/like) باغ بهشت ('a garden of Paradise' — noun + attributive noun). یکسر is an adverb meaning 'wholly/entirely'.
segments.s220-g358: بدیدار ایشان functions adverbially as 'at the sight of them' (بدیدار = 'in seeing/at the sight'), ایشان = 'them'; شده is the perfect/become-type verb 'became' and خوب زشت is a compressed poetic antonym pair literally 'good (خوب) [to] ugly (زشت)'. The phrase means that upon seeing them what was fair appeared ugly — a deliberate terse contrast used in epic diction.
segments.s220-g359: بران here is the archaic/prepositional form meaning 'upon that' or 'on it'; کوه is 'mountain' and سر ماند literally 'the head remained' where سر = 'summit/top' and ماند = past 'remained/was left'. The sequence places the locative phrase first and then states that 'Rostam remained (there) astonished' — شگفت functions as a predicate adjective 'astonished'.
segments.s220-g360: ببرگشتن is the masdar/infinitive 'to return' (with the verbal prefix b- attached as in classical Persian); اندیشه 'the thought' is its noun; اندر گرفت is a compound meaning 'seized/possessed (him)' — literally 'took hold inside'. The whole clause reads 'the thought of returning seized him.' Word order places the infinitive before اندیشه for emphasis.
segments.s220-g393: همه precedes the listed nouns and means 'all' (collective). The noun phrase تیغ و گرز و کمند is an asyndetic/coordinated list joined by و, and the verb آورید (imperative, 2nd-person plural/formal) comes at the end — typical classical Persian word order where the verb follows its objects. آورید here is a command 'bring' (do bring).
segments.s220-g394: بایرانیان is the plural group being affected (object/topic); تنگ و بند form a coordinated predicate meaning 'narrow/tight and fetters/bonds' functioning as the result to be brought upon them. Again آورید is the imperative verb at the end: 'make/bring the Bayranians into tightness and bonds' = 'confine and bind them.'
segments.s220-g395: جهانجوی را دل is a possessive/topic construction: the postposition/marker را attaches to جهانجوی to mark the possessor/topic ('the world-seeker's'). دل is the possessed noun. بجنگ is a prepositional/infinitival complement meaning 'for battle / in fighting' and اندرست is the archaic stative verb 'is within/placed in.' Together: 'The world-seeker's heart is in/for battle.'
segments.s220-g396: وگرنه is a connective meaning 'otherwise.' سرش is سر + enclitic pronoun ش (his), standard possessive/clitic attachment. زیر سنگ is a prepositional phrase 'under a stone' and اندرست again is the archaic copular/stative 'is/lies.' The clause means 'otherwise his head is under a stone' (i.e. crushed/dead).
segments.s220-g113: ازان is an archaic/poetic compound of از + آن meaning 'of those' (literally 'from those'); it introduces a partitive/group noun phrase. نامدارن is a plural substantive 'the renowned/celebrated', and گودرزیان is a plural proper‑name identifying the group (the Gudarzis) — together they form a nominal phrase: 'of those renowned Gudarzis'.
segments.s220-g114: که is the relative pronoun 'who/which/that' linking this clause to the preceding noun phrase. از here marks cause ('because of' / 'on account of') and introduces the reason phrase کینه جستن, where جستن is the infinitive 'to seek' used in a poetic verb phrase meaning 'seeking/engaging in (a) grudge/vengeance'. سرآمد is a predicate meaning 'foremost' and زمان ('time/age') completes the superlative: 'foremost of the age'.
segments.s220-g115: بدو ('to him') is an enclitic-style indirect object placed before the verb, a frequent word‑order in epic Persian; گفت here is the verb 'said' (past) with the speaker following (گودرز). کای is the vocative particle 'O', used to call the addressee, and پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero' being addressed.
segments.s220-g116: A string of epithets linked by و: هشیوار 'alert/awake', جنگی 'warlike/fighter', روشن‌روان a compound epithet 'bright‑soul/clear‑minded'. In Persian poetry such adjectival epithets follow the vocative and collectively describe the addressed hero.
segments.s220-g117: همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'continually/at times' (here marking a habitual or characteristic action). The coordinate تاج و گاه pairs 'the crown' with a temporal adverbial 'گاه' ('at times'); از تو marks source ('from you') and گیرد is an archaic/present‑habitual verb form 'takes'/'draws', with فروغ 'radiance/glory' as the thing taken (object). Word order is poetically flexible: adverbial and subject precede the verb.
segments.s220-g118: سخن begins a free relative clause: 'سخن هرچ گویی' = 'whatever words you say'. گویی here is the present/subjunctive form 'you say' (poetic usage). نباشد is a negative jussive/existential 'is not' and is followed by the predicate دروغ 'a lie/falsehood' — together: 'whatever you say is not a lie.'
segments.s220-g119: تو is the addressed 'you' (subject/vocative). ایرانیان را uses را in a partitive/definite sense 'of the Iranians' (marking the group being compared). ز (poetic form of از) before مام و پدر indicates origin/by birth ('by mother and father'), so the whole phrase says he excels among Iranians by parentage.
segments.s220-g120: بهی is a predicative adjective 'excellent/best' that relates back to تو; هم 'also/likewise' extends the praise to other domains. ز + noun repeatedly ('ز گنج و ز تخت و گهر') lists sources/criteria ('by treasure, by throne, and by jewel'), giving parallel grounds on which he is superior.
segments.s220-g137: ازان پس چو آگاه شد طوس و گیو is a temporal clause: ازان پس + چو = 'after that, when'; آگاه شد is a two-word predicate ('became aware', past tense) with آگاه (adjective/participial) + شد (verb 'become'). طوس و گیو follow as the logical subject (P1 and P2 listed after the verb phrase, a common epic ordering).
segments.s220-g138: ز ایران نبرده سواران نیو is the content clause explaining what they learned. ز ایران marks source ('from Iran'); نبرده is a perfective negative ('has not taken' / 'did not take') governing the object سواران ('horsemen, riders'); نیو here is a proper name (agent) and does not match the dictionary senses given, so it is treated as an archaic/name token.
segments.s220-g139: که introduces the clause ('that/when' used as a connective here). رستم به کوه هماون رسید is straightforward: به marks motion toward/location, کوه هماون is the goal ('the Homāvan mountain'), and رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s220-g140: مر او را جهاندیده گودرز دید is ordered with the dative/indirect clitic مر ('to/for me' or a narrative dative) first, then the direct object او را ('him' + object marker). جهاندیده (an epithet 'world‑wise' / 'experienced') immediately precedes the name گودرز which is the agent, and دید is the past verb 'saw'. The overall sense: 'Goudarz the world‑wise saw him.'
segments.s220-g141: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/advanced'. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying the manner of departure. لشکر ز جای places the noun 'the army' and the origin phrase 'from their place' (ز جای) after the simile — the epic permits this flexible order.
segments.s220-g142: خروش آمد is a simple existential/past clause 'a roar came/arose'. و links the next noun phrase ناله کرنای, where ناله ('wail/cry') governs کرنای ('of the horn/trumpet') in a genitive relation (i.e. 'the trumpet's wailing' or 'the wailing blast of the trumpet').
segments.s220-g143: چو آمد = 'when it came/appeared'. درفش تهمتن پدید is a compact clause where درفش تهمتن ('the banner of Tahmatan' — epithet + name) is the nominative theme and پدید ('appeared, became visible') is the verb; the noun + epithet before the verb is a frequent poetic order.
segments.s220-g144: شب تیره لشکر برستم رسید: شب تیره is the compound 'dark night'. لشکر is the subject ('the host/army'), رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached'. برستم here is an archaic/poetic locative/target form referring to Rostam (i.e. 'upon/at Rostam' or 'to Rostam') and so the clause means roughly 'the host's dark night reached/overcame Rostam.' The form برستم does not match the provided dictionary senses and is treated as an inflected/archaic target form.
segments.s220-g65: بنیزه is an adverbial/circumstantial phrase 'with a spear' placed before the verb; درآمد is a past-tense intransitive verb 'entered/charged in'. بکردار is an adjective attributive to گرگ ('a wolf of deed' / 'a wolf in action') and follows the verb, so the whole phrase describes the manner/character of the charger.
segments.s220-g66: چو here is the comparative/simile 'like' (introducing a simile). شیری is the adjectival form 'leonine' (like a lion). برافراز is an infinitive/imperative-type verbal root used descriptively 'raising' or 'erecting'. پیلی سترگ is a compound adjective phrase ('yellow/pale (پیلی) eye/sight (سترگ)') describing the lifted gaze; the adjective follows the noun as in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s220-g67: چو آمد is the common temporal clause 'when ... came'. بنزدیک is an adverb 'near, close'. بدخواه اوی is a noun phrase 'his ill-wisher/enemy' where اوی is the archaic possessive clitic 'his' positioned after the noun (typical older Persian).
segments.s220-g68: یکی functions here as an indefinite 'one' / 'a certain man'. نیزه is the direct object 'spear'. زد is the past-tense transitive 'struck/ thrust'. بر is the preposition 'at/on/against' introducing the target (کمرگاه 'waist, small of the back'). اوی again is the possessive 'his'.
segments.s220-g69: چو شد is the temporal 'when (it) became / when ... did'. گیو is the proper name (subject). جنبان is a participial/adjectival form 'moving/active' describing Giv, and بزین (بزین) is a verbal infinitive/past form 'to saddle up / he mounted (the horse)'; اندرون gives direction 'inward' or 'into the midst'. The clause sequence is typical poetic inversion: state/time → subject → participle → verb → direction.
segments.s220-g70: ازو دور شد is a short clause 'from him became distant' i.e. 'moved away from him'. نیزه is the noun 'spear' and آبگون is an adjectival epithet 'bluish/pale' modifying نیزه. The verb شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became/went' governing the change of state.
segments.s220-g71: سبک here functions adverbially 'lightly' or 'with a light motion'. تیغ را uses the object marker را to mark 'the sword' as definite/direct object. برکشید is the past transitive 'drew up / drew (a weapon)'. از نیام specifies source 'out of the scabbard' (نیام = scabbard).
segments.s220-g72: خروشید and جوشید are sequential intransitive verbs describing his reaction: 'he roared' (خروشید) and 'he seethed/swelled/erupted with emotion' (جوشید); the repeated و is simple coordination 'and'. برگفت نام is a verb + object 'he spoke/uttered the name' (برگفت = uttered/said; نام = name).
segments.s220-g121: چنانیم = ‘I/we am/are thus’ (present/poetic copular idea); بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase ‘without you’ placed after it for emphasis. چو introduces the simile ‘like/as,’ followed by ماهی ‘fish’ as the comparison term; بخاک is a directional/locative phrase ‘to the ground / onto the earth’ completing the simile. The verb ‘am’ is contained in چنانیم, so the rest are its complements.
segments.s220-g122: This is a compact, poetic sequence of prepositional/nominal phrases describing physical and existential distress. بتنگ modifies the following اندرون ‘within/inside’ (i.e. cramped/pressed within), اندرون سر = ‘within the head/skull’; then تن اندر هلاک inverts the expected order (typical in poetry) to mean ‘the body is (or lies) in doom/destruction.’ There is no overt copula; the predicates are understood.
segments.s220-g123: چو here is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; دیدم is the 1st-person past ‘I saw’ (so the clause reads ‘when I saw…’). کنون is the adverb ‘now/presently’; خوب‌چهر ترا = ‘your fair-faced (person)’ with the enclitic ت marking the 2nd person and را as the object marker attached to ترا. Word order places the adverb after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s220-g124: همین ‘this/just’ qualifies پرسش ‘question’ and گرم gives the sense ‘ardent/keen’ (not merely temperature). و links to مهر ‘love/affection’; ت (enclitic) again marks the 2nd person possessor/object and را is the object marker. In Persian poetry an adjective like گرم can modify پرسش to mean ‘an ardent question.’
segments.s220-g125: مرا سوگ آن ارجمندان نماند = ‘To me the mourning for those esteemed ones did not remain.’ مرا is the dative/affected-object ‘to me’ (here split into tokens in the annotation), سوگ ‘mourning’ is the theme, آن ارجمندان ‘those esteemed ones’ is the genitive/possessive phrase specifying whose mourning, and نماند is the past negative ‘did not remain.’ Word order is topic/affected-object + theme + modifier + verb.
segments.s220-g126: ببخت تو = an epithet ‘wretched/ill-fated you’ (adjective before the pronoun for force). جز = ‘except/but’; روی خندان = ‘(your) smiling face’ (noun + adjective), and نماند is again the past negative ‘did not remain/was left.’ The whole clause means ‘for your ill fortune nothing remained except your smiling face.’
segments.s220-g127: بدو = ‘to him/unto him’ (dative) and introduces the reported speech. گفت رستم = ‘Rostam said’ (the order in the text places the verb before the name for poetic rhythm). که introduces the content of the speech. دل شاد دار is an imperative-like instruction ‘keep (your) heart glad’ where دل = heart, شاد = glad, and دار is the imperative/command ‘keep’ (poetic/older verbal form).
segments.s220-g128: ز غمهای گیتی = prepositional phrase ‘from the sorrows of the world’ (ز = from, غمهای = sorrows, گیتی = world). سر آزاد دار is parallel to the previous imperative: سر ‘head/mind’, آزاد ‘free’, دار ‘keep’ — literally ‘keep the head free (from…)’. The two imperatives form Rostam’s advice: keep your heart joyful and your head free from worldly griefs.
segments.s220-g225: بیامد is a past narrative verb meaning 'he came/arrived' (3rd-person past). پر + از + غم is the idiomatic adjective phrase 'full of sorrow' where پر ('full') is followed by the preposition از + noun. بپیران is به + پیران: the prefix بـ here marks motion/toward and پیران is the plural 'elders', so 'to the elders'. بگفت is the past-tense narrative 'he said' (3rd-person), introducing reported speech.
segments.s220-g226: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'). شد is the past form 'became' (here a full verb). روز is the grammatical subject 'the day'. با رنج بسیار is a prepositional phrase 'with much suffering' (با = 'with', رنج = 'suffering', بسیار = 'much'). جفت is used predicatively — literally 'pair/paired' — here meaning 'matched or joined (with suffering)', forming the predicate with شد.
segments.s220-g227: کز is the archaic/poetic contraction meaning 'from' (essentially 'که از'), marking origin. ایران is the place 'Iran'. The coordinating و joins nouns (ده/دار — 'village(s) and houses/dwellings'); بانگ خروش is a compound noun phrase 'the cry/voice (بانگ) of tumult/uproar (خروش)'. The phrase lists things coming from/occurring in Iran.
segments.s220-g228: فراوان here qualifies the preceding list: 'abundant / plentiful'. ز is the poetic form of 'از' and in comparative contexts often marks 'than' or 'from'; هر شب فزون بود دوش is a comparative statement: هر شب = 'any/each night', فزون = 'greater/more', بود = past 'was', دوش = 'last night'. So the clause says 'last night it was greater than any night.'
segments.s220-g229: بتنها = به + تنها, an adverbial phrase meaning 'alone'/'only'. برفتم is a 1st-person past form 'I went/ I set out' (verb root رفتن with past ending -م); in classical narrative the b- prefix may appear in some forms, but grammatically this is the speaker's past action. ز خیمه is the prepositional phrase 'from the tent' (ز = 'from'). پگاه is an adverbial noun 'dawn / early morning' marking time.
segments.s220-g230: بلشکر is به + لشکر: the prefix بـ marks accompaniment ('with'), so 'with the army/host'. بهر is a classical preposition meaning 'toward / for', here 'toward' the target. جای is the noun 'place'. کردم is 1st-person past 'I did/made' used as a light verb; combined with نگاه ('a look, gaze') it yields the idiom 'کردم نگاه' = 'I cast/looked (my gaze)'. نگاه is the object 'gaze / glance'.
segments.s220-g231: از ایران is the origin phrase 'from Iran'. فراوان modifies سپاه: 'a plentiful/large army/host'. آمدست is an older narrative/perfect-past form meaning 'has come' (آمد + است fused in epic diction), indicating completed arrival.
segments.s220-g232: بیاری here corresponds to به یاری / 'with aid/companions' (in epic usage it can mean 'with companions' or 'with support'); gramatically it functions adverbially 'with [others]'. بر + ین composes برین, a demonstrative/locative formation meaning 'to this / upon this' (بر = 'on/against/toward' + ین ~ این 'this'), so برین رزمگاه = 'to this battlefield'. آمدست repeats the perfect-past 'has come.'
segments.s220-g49: که introduces a relative/nominal clause ('that/which'). این precedes the noun لشکری (demonstrative + noun). گشن and کنداورست are coordinated predicates/adjectives modifying لشکری (joined by و); the clause has an archaic descriptive feel (کنداورست is a compound/old-form adjective). Word order is demonstrative + noun + predicate adjectives, with the copular sense implicit.
segments.s220-g50: نه is a contrastive negator ('not'). It precedes a coordination of nouns (پیران و هومان و آن ...) and the final element لشکرست contains the old/copular suffix -ست, so the predicate/coping sense ('is a host') is realized on the rightmost word and applies to the contrasted list. Word order: negator + coordinated noun phrase + predicate-marking element.
segments.s220-g51: که again begins a relative clause ('that/who'). دارید is a present-tense verb meaning 'you have' (subject 'you' is omitted). ز introduces the source 'from', so ز ایرانیان جنگجوی = 'warriors from the Iranians' (preposition + noun phrase). The noun جنگجوی follows ایرانیان and specifies the kind of men.
segments.s220-g52: که here resumes the relative marker ('who/that'). با من is a prepositional phrase 'with me' (من is object 'me'). بروی is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'go' (infinitive/subjunctive flavor); اندر is a locative/prepositional 'into/among'; آرند is 3rd-person plural present 'they bring' or 'they cause to come'; روی is the noun 'face' (used idiomatically 'bring their faces' = present themselves). Together this expresses 'who will go with me and bring/bring forward their faces'.
segments.s220-g53: ببینید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'look' or 'see'. بالا و برز form a coordinated nominal phrase 'height/loftiness and exaltation' that functions as the object/topic of the imperative. مرا is the first-person marker in classical usage attached after the noun phrase to indicate possession ('my …'); را is the object/definite marker emphasizing that phrase.
segments.s220-g54: برو here is another imperative-like or exhortative verb ('go' / 'behold' in an attention-getting sense). بازوی و تیغ و گرز is a coordinated list 'arm and sword and mace' naming instruments of strength; again مرا (with را) follows to mark those items as possessed by the speaker ('my arm, my sword, my mace'). Word order is verb + coordinated objects + possessive/object marker.
segments.s220-g55: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd-person singular). The proper name گیو follows (classical Persian permits subject after the verb), and این سخن is the direct object 'these words'. بردمید is a past-form verb here meaning 'he seized/rose up' (an abrupt action following hearing); it continues into the next coordinated clause.
segments.s220-g56: برآشفت is past tense 'he became angry/was agitated' (3rd sing). و coordinates the next action. تیغ is the object 'sword'; از میان is a prepositional phrase 'from the middle/from among' describing where the sword came from; برکشید is a past 3rd-person verb 'he drew/lifted' (here 'drew his sword'). The subject is the same (Giv) carried over from the prior clause.
segments.s220-g249: بیاری فراوان is an adverbial phrase 'with abundant aid/support' (بیاری = with helpers/aid, فراوان = abundant) placed before the subject. سپاه is the subject 'the army/host' and آمدست is the classical past/perfective third‑person form 'has come' (آمد + است contracted to آمدست). Word order here is adverbial phrase + subject + verb, typical of poetic/classical inversion.
segments.s220-g250: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' modifying the following compound description. کینه‌ور رزمخواه is a compound nominal phrase 'those bearing hatred, desirous of battle' (کینه‌ور = bearing grudge/hostile, رزمخواه = war‑seeking/fighter), and آمدست again is the past 'has come.' The whole segment = 'many grudge‑bearing, battle‑seeking people have come.'
segments.s220-g251: گمانم is the predicative noun 'my thought' with the 1st‑person enclitic -م and functions as 'I think / I suppose.' که introduces a subordinate clause 'that.' آن رستم پیلتن is a demonstrative + name + epithet: 'that Rostam of Piltan' (آن marking the specific person).
segments.s220-g252: The second که is a relative conjunction 'whom/that' introducing the relative clause modifying رستم. گفتم is the past 'I said / I mentioned' (here: 'whom I mentioned'); همی is a classical emphatic/adverbial 'just/indeed' (here 'just/just now'); پیش این انجمن is the locative/existential phrase 'before/in the presence of this assembly' (پیش = before/in front of, این انجمن = this assembly).
segments.s220-g253: برفت is a classical past 'he/it went away / departed.' از در = 'from the gate' (از = from, در = gate/door). شاه ایران is a genitive construction 'the Shah of Iran.' سپاه is the subject 'the army.' Note the verb appears before the prepositional phrase and subject — an inversion common in epic/classical style.
segments.s220-g254: بیاری is again 'with aid/companions' used adverbially; بیامد is the simple past 'came.' بدین (here split as ب + دین) means 'to this' — ب (prefix/to) + دین (here part of the demonstrative 'دین' in the text, i.e. 'this') — forming 'to this.' رزمگاه = 'battlefield.' So the clause reads 'with aid came to this battlefield.'
segments.s220-g255: بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase 'to him' (to him/unto him). گفت is the past 'said.' کاموس is the speaker's name (here the subject). کای is a vocative/title addressing the king 'O Kay'; پر خرد is an epithet 'full of wisdom' (پر = full (of), خرد = wisdom), with the adjective‑like element پر preceding the noun خرد.
segments.s220-g256: دلت is دل + enclitic -ت 'your heart' (possessive enclitic). یکسر is an adverb 'completely / wholly.' اندیشه بد is the noun phrase 'evil/bad thought' (اندیشه = thought, بد = bad) and برد is the past of بردن 'to carry/take (away)' used here causatively/privatively 'has taken away / has carried off' — 'your heart has been wholly carried away by an evil thought.'
segments.s220-g41: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. کاموس here is a proper name (a warrior) and is the subject; its lexical senses list did not include a proper-name sense. تنگ is an adjective/noun 'narrow' used as a location (the narrow pass). اندر آمد is a past tense compound meaning 'entered/ came into' (اندر = 'into', آمد = 'came'). بجنگ is an infinitive-like construction 'to fight / into battle' that complements آمد ('entered to fight').
segments.s220-g42: بهامون is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for us' (به + ما with colloquial form). زمانی here means 'for a while / time' (temporal duration). نبودش is past negative 'there was not / he did not have' (نبود + enclitic -ش referring back to the time/ability). درنگ is a noun 'delay, tarrying' and is the object of نبودش: 'there was no delay/time to tarry.'
segments.s220-g43: سپه را uses را as a marker that makes 'the army' definite or emphatic. بکردار is a poetic/archaic compound (به‌کردار) meaning 'in the guise/likeness of' (not the modern sense 'virtuous' in the supplied list). دریای آب is a genitive phrase 'sea of water' describing that likeness; the head noun دریای (دریا) 'sea' takes آب 'water' as its complement.
segments.s220-g44: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which / that'. از کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the mountain' (از = from, کوه = mountain). سیل 'flood/torrent' is the subject of the verb. اندر آید is present/future-like 'comes in / pours forth' (اندر = into/on, آید = comes). شتاب at the end is a noun/adverb 'haste' modifying the verb (i.e. 'comes with haste').
segments.s220-g45: بیاورد is a past form 'brought' or 'brought about/caused' — in narrative it indicates that the flood/force 'came' or 'brought itself' forward. و is the coordinating 'and'. پیش here means 'in front of / before'. هماون is a proper name (Humāvān/Homāvon) in context and functions as the referent before which something arrived; the supplied senses list treats it as demonstrative but here it's a name. رسید is the past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s220-g46: هوا 'the air/sky' is the subject. نیلگون is an adjective 'azure, indigo‑colored' and شد is the past of شدن 'became' (the sky became indigo). زمین 'the earth' is the subject of the second clause and ناپدید is a predicate adjective/participle 'vanished, gone' — two short clauses joined without an explicit conjunction (a common poetic coordination).
segments.s220-g47: چو is 'when' (temporal). نزدیک شد is a fused verbal phrase 'drew near / approached' (نزدیک = near, شد = became/was — idiomatically 'approached'). سر 'head' is the direct object of کرد here in the sense 'he turned his head' (سوی کوه کرد = 'he made [his] head toward the mountain'). سوی کوه کرد is a verbal phrase 'turned toward the mountain' with کرد as the main verb 'made/did (turn)'.
segments.s220-g48: پر از خنده رخ is a noun phrase 'face full of laughter' (پر = 'full (of)', از = the linking preposition 'of', خنده = 'laughter', رخ = 'face'). سوی انبوه کرد is parallel to the previous clause: سوی = 'toward', انبوه = 'multitude/throng' (here 'the assembled crowd'), and کرد is the verb 'he turned / made [his face] toward' — the sequence describes his smiling face as he faced the crowd.
segments.s220-g185: تن ما بتو زنده شد بی‌گمان is a clause with the patient/possessed phrase (تنِ ما, “my/our body”) first, the prepositional/agent phrase بتو (classical contraction of به تو, here 'by you') immediately after, and the verbal predicate زنده شد ('became alive') at the end; شد is the past-tense verb 'became' rather than a simple copula and زنده is a predicate adjective. بی‌گمان is a sentence-final adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause. Word order (possessed noun + agent + predicate) is typical of older Persian poetic style.
segments.s220-g186: نبد here is an archaic contracted negative past (equivalent to نبود 'there was not'); it appears before the remainder of the clause. هیچ‌کس را امید زمان literally lists the experiencer/affected person (هیچ‌کس) plus the object/thing (امید زمان 'hope of/time') with را marking the object/definite phrase. The overall structure means 'there was no hope for anyone in (the) time.'
segments.s220-g187: ازان کشتگان یک زمان پهلوان = 'from those/the slain, for a time a champion' — ازان ('of those/among them') selects from the previously mentioned slain, کشتگان is the plural noun 'the slain', یک زمان functions adverbially as 'once/for a time', and پهلوان is the nominal head (the champion). The order places the modifying/locating phrases before the noun, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s220-g188: همی بود گریان و تیره‌روان uses همی + بود to mark a past continuous/state (همی is a habitual/continuous marker before the past copula), so 'there was/there remained' or 'was then'. گریان is a predicative participle 'weeping', and تیره‌روان is a compound adjective 'gloomy/ill‑fated', joined by و. The copula (بود) links the subject پهلوان of the previous segment to these predicates.
segments.s220-g189: ازان پس چنین گفت کز چرخ ماه = 'After that he said thus: that/from which (or: that) — the phrase ازان پس 'after that' opens the reported speech; چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said'. کز is the archaic relative/conjunction 'that/from which', introducing what follows. چرخ here is a celestial term (the sky/wheel of heaven) and ماه is 'the moon'; together they locate the starting point of the imperative that follows.
segments.s220-g190: برو تا سر تیره خاک سیاه is an imperative clause: برو 'go' (2nd‑person imperative) followed by تا marking the endpoint 'as far as/until'. سر 'head/top' is the noun being reached, modified by تیره 'dark' and then further qualified by خاک سیاه 'black earth' (an adjectival/genitival cluster). The sequence places the verb first (imperative), then the endpoint phrase introduced by تا.
segments.s220-g191: نبینی مگر گرم و تیمار و رنج is a negative-present (classical) clause: نبینی ('you will not see' / 'you do not see') plus مگر meaning 'except' or 'nothing but'. The enumerated nouns گرم (here 'ardor/zeal'), تیمار ('care/solicitude') and رنج ('suffering/hardship') are the only things that will be seen. In older Persian نبینی can act like a future or general present in contexts like this.
segments.s220-g192: برینست رسم سرای سپنج is an instance of an older fused formula: برینست represents بر این است ('this is the case' / 'such is'), followed by رسم 'custom' and سرای 'house/home' used as a genitive construction; سپنج names the place or household (literal sense 'sponge' in the supplied senses, but here likely a proper or technical name). So the phrase means 'such is the custom of the house/household of Sapanj.'
segments.s220-g169: فراوان (faraavān) is a quantifier placed before the noun phrase and here means “many” or “numerous.” ازان is the poetic contraction of ازِ آن (az-e ān) and marks a partitive/relational link “of/from that,” so لشکر بی‌شمار (= “laskar bi-shomār”) is the noun phrase “that countless host/army,” with the adjective بی‌شمار following the noun as is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s220-g170: بگفتند is the past 3rd-person plural of گفتن with the classical prefix ب- (here functioning as the past narrative), meaning “they said/spoke.” با (with) marks accompaniment: با مهتر نامدار means “with the famed/renowned chief,” where مهتر (headman/chief) is the head noun and نامدار is a postpositive adjective (“renowned”). The clause thus reads “they spoke together with the renowned chief.”
segments.s220-g171: The segment uses the repeated poetic preposition ز (az, “from/of”) to enumerate sources or names. کاموس and شنگل are treated as proper names here (classical/obscure names), and خاقان چین is a compound meaning “the khaghan of China,” with چین (China) postpositive. Word order is typical enumerative Persian: ز + Name, و + Name, ز + Title + Place.
segments.s220-g172: Again ز introduces a noun phrase “from …”: منشور جنگی pairs منشور (a charter/inscribed tablet/manifesto) with the postpositive adjective جنگی (“warlike”/“military”), and then و links it to مردان کین, where مردان is the plural noun “men” and کین is a postpositive noun meaning “vengeance/hatred,” i.e. “men bent on vengeance.” The structure lists items/people related to war.
segments.s220-g173: ز here introduces a topic/subject (“regarding Kamus”). کاموس is a proper name; خود is emphatic ‘himself/own’; جای گفتار is a frozen noun phrase “place/room for speech,” and نیست is the copula negation “is not/there is not.” Together the segment says that, regarding Kamus himself, there is no space to speak (i.e. he needs no more words).
segments.s220-g174: که introduces an explanatory clause (here effectively “for”/“because”), ما را uses ما (we/us) with the object/definite marker را (emphatic/accusative), بدو = به + او ‘to him,’ and راه دیدار is a compound “way/path of meeting.” نیست negates the existence: “we have no way to meet him.”
segments.s220-g175: درختیست is the fused poetic form of درخت است (“it is a tree”), serving as the predicate. بارش is بار + ـش (the noun ‘load/fruit/yield’ with the possessive/attached pronoun -sh “its”), so بارش همه = “all its fruit” or “whose every yield”; گرز و تیغ lists those yields (“mace and sword”), with the adjectives/nouns following in the typical Persian order. The clause describes a tree whose fruits are weapons.
segments.s220-g176: نترسد is the negative present (or timeless) form of ترسیدن, “it does not fear.” اگر introduces a conditional (“if”), سنگ بارد is an older/poetic verb form where بارد here functions as ‘falls/rains’ (i.e. ‘stones fall’); ز میغ is a prepositional phrase “from the blade.” So the clause means “it fears not if stones fall from the blade.” Note: بارد is an archaic/pedantic present 3rd-singular of باریدن and thus differs from the modern adjectival readings listed in the lemma entry.
segments.s220-g81: عنان را بپیچید is a past tense transitive verb phrase: بپیچید (پیچید + verbal prefix بـ) is 3rd person singular past 'twisted/turned/tightened' and را marks عنان (the object) as definite. کاموس follows as the subject (word order V‑O‑S here, poetic), and تنگ is an adverbial/qualifier meaning 'tightly/close'.
segments.s220-g82: میان دو گرد is a prepositional phrase 'between the two گرد' (میان = 'between'); اندر آمد is past 'he came/entered' with اندر = 'into/within' + آمد 'came'; بجنگ is an infinitive/ purpose phrase 'to fight' (literally 'came in to fight'). The clause places the movement (آمد) toward the purpose (بجنگ).
segments.s220-g83: ز introduces a source/circumstance ('from' / 'with'); تگ اسپ is a compound meaning 'a single/lone horse' (تگ = 'single'); طوس دلاور is the name with epithet 'Tūs the brave' (apposition), and بماند is 3rd person singular past 'remained / was left'. The phrase describes that Tūs was left with/at a single horse.
segments.s220-g84: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed'; برو here is a narrative motion verb 'went' / 'went forward' (simple past/in narrative sense); نام یزدان بخواند is a compound verb phrase where نام یزدان = 'the name of God' and بخواند (3rd pers. past) means 'he called/summoned/ invoked' — together 'he invoked the name of God.'
segments.s220-g85: به نیزه پیاده is a cluster of modifiers: به + نیزه + پیاده — 'with/at the spear, on foot' (here indicating he was armed with a spear and on foot); the following به آوردگاه uses به = 'to' and آوردگاه = 'battlefield/place of bringing', so the whole phrase indicates movement toward the battlefield while armed and on foot.
segments.s220-g86: همی گشت is a habitual/continuous past narrative form: همی (continuative particle) + گشت (past of گشتن) 'kept/was going'; با او = 'with him'; بپیش سپاه = 'toward the front of the army' (بپیش = 'toward/forward', سپاه = 'army'). The clause thus says he continued/moved with that person toward the army's front.
segments.s220-g87: دو گرد گرانمایه و یک سوار lists participants: دو گرد = 'two گرد' (a unit/round/company – here a poetic military unit), گرانمایه = an epithet 'precious/esteemed' modifying گرد, و = 'and', یک سوار = 'one rider/mounted man'. Word order is noun + adjective + conjunction + noun phrase.
segments.s220-g88: کشانی نشد سیر زان کارزار is a negative result clause: کشانی (an adjectival/nominal form here — contextually 'mounted/cavalry' or a classifier attached to the rider) is the predicate/topic; نشد = 'did not become' (negated past auxiliary), سیر = 'sated'/'satisfaction' and زان کارزار = 'from that battle' (زان = 'from that', کارزار = 'battle'). Together: they did not become sated/from that battle (i.e. the fight did not satiate/tire them).
segments.s220-g313: ز is the poetic form of از ‘from, of’ and marks origin or association. کاموس here is likely a proper name (not one of the listed senses), so I mark it as a name; چون + noun + شد is a simile construction: چون کوه شد = “became like a mountain,” with شد as the past-tense verb ‘became.’ میمنه is a noun meaning ‘the right side/wing’ and is the subject that ‘became like a mountain.’
segments.s220-g314: کشیدند is the 3rd-person plural past of کشیدن ‘to pull/draw’ used here in the sense ‘they moved/drew (their forces).’ بر سوی is a double directional expression ‘toward’ (بر + سوی) that governs the following place, هامون. هامون is a common epic-place noun ‘the plain / marshland.’ بنه is unclear here (likely a place-name or an epithet attached to هامون) and does not fit the provided senses.
segments.s220-g315: سوی = ‘toward’ marks direction. میسره (likely a place-name here) does not match the listed abstract senses and is treated as a destination. نیز = ‘also’ attaches this movement to a previous one. پیران is the plural of پیر here meaning ‘elders’ or senior men/commanders who are the subject that برفت (went). Note: برفت is an archaic/poetic past form of رفت ‘went.’
segments.s220-g316: برادرش = برادر + -ش ‘his brother’ (possessive clitic). هومان is a proper name in apposition; و = ‘and.’ کلباد is another proper name. تفت here appears as a name or epithet attached to کلباد (not the verbal senses given), so it is treated as non‑lexical in the provided list.
segments.s220-g317: چو here = ‘when’ (temporal conjunction). رستم is the subject. بدید is the past tense ‘saw.’ آنک (آن + کِه) = ‘that which’ or ‘what’ introducing the content of Rostam’s perception: آنک خاقان چه کرد = ‘what the khaghan had done.’ خاقان is the title ‘khaghan/sovereign.’ چه کرد is an embedded clause ‘what did (he) do,’ with کرد as the past-tense verb.
segments.s220-g318: بیاراست is a past verb form of آراستن ‘to adorn/prepare’; here it means ‘he prepared/fitted out.’ در = ‘in’; قلب = ‘heart’ (metaphorical core); جای = ‘place’ (object of بیاراست); نبرد = ‘battle.’ The whole phrase means he prepared a place for battle at/in the heart (i.e., made ready internally or at the core of the conflict).
segments.s220-g319: چنین = ‘thus/so,’ introducing direct speech. گفت here is the lexical verb ‘said’ but the provided senses list did not include the verb reading (so it is marked as not matching the supplied senses). رستم names the speaker. که following گفت introduces the content clause (that/which): گردان سپهر = ‘the troop/host of the heavens’ (گردان = troop; سپهر = heavens/heavenly).
segments.s220-g320: ببینیم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/imperfect of دیدن ‘let us see / may we see’ (a hortatory/inquisitive use: ‘let us observe’). تا here functions as a purposive conjunction ‘so that/in order that.’ بر = ‘toward’ and که = interrogative ‘whom/which’ forming بر که = ‘to whom.’ گردد is the subjunctive/present of گردیدن ‘turn/become’ (here ‘turns to’), and بمهر = ‘with favor/affection’ (i.e., ‘in favor’). Together: “let us see so that we may know to whom it will turn in favor.”
segments.s220-g265: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The verb آیم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (classical b‑form) meaning 'I come/arrive', and من is the subject (appearing after the verb for poetic inversion). با سپاه اندر (with + army + in/into) marks accompaniment and location; بجنگ (= به جنگ) is an infinitival/locative phrase 'into/for battle' (not an imperative here).
segments.s220-g266: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'it must not / one should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ... be', and باشد is the subjunctive/jussive form 'be' (here 'that there be'). شما را uses را as an object/experiencer marker 'for you', and درنگ is the noun 'delay / hesitation' — so the clause literally says 'that there be for you delay' → 'you must not delay'.
segments.s220-g267: ببینی is the present/future second‑person form (b‑prefixed) 'you see / you will see'; the subject pronoun تو follows the verb by poetic inversion for emphasis. پیکار مردان is a nominal phrase 'the battle of the men' (genitive relation expressed by juxtaposition), and کنون is the adverb 'now' placed at the end.
segments.s220-g268: شده is the perfect/passive participle 'has become'. دشت is the subject 'the plain', modified by یکسر 'entirely/utterly'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and دریای خون is a possessive/construct phrase 'sea of blood' (دریا + خون) — together: 'the plain has become entirely like a sea of blood'.
segments.s220-g269: دل پهلوان is a possessive/construct phrase 'the heart of the champion' (subject). زان is a contracted poetic preposition 'from/by that' indicating cause ('by that speech'), سخن is 'speech/words', شاد is predicate adjective 'glad', and گشت is past 'became' — literally 'the champion's heart became glad because of that speech'.
segments.s220-g270: ز is the preposition 'from / because of' marking cause; اندیشه here means 'thought / anxiety'. رستم is the subject, followed by the predicate آزاد (free) and the past verb گشت 'became' — so literally 'Rostam became free from thought', i.e. relieved of anxiety.
segments.s220-g271: سپه را places 'the army' as the definite object (را marking) before the verb by poetic inversion. همه quantifies 'all', followed by ترگ 'rags' and جوشن 'armor/curiass' joined by و 'and'. بداد is intended as the past verb 'he gave' in context (poetic compound), though the lemma senses provided do not list that sense here; syntactically it is the past action 'he gave (to the army) all rags and armor'.
segments.s220-g272: همی + کرد is a habitual/continuous past construction 'he used to / he kept on' (همی = continually), with کرد 'did/made' functioning as the main verb. گفتار is 'utterance / words', and کاموس یاد appears as a quoted phrase or name (treated here as a fixed expression); یاد is 'memory / mention'. The clause thus means 'he kept/was wont to utter the words “Kāmos‑yād”' (the exact lexical identity of کاموس is a proper/name sense not given in the supplied senses).
segments.s220-g89: برین گونه is a poetic fusion of بر + این + گونه meaning 'in this manner' or 'thus'. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'; تیره شد is past tense 'became dark' (تیره = dark, شد = became). جای هور is a genitive phrase 'the place of Hūr' (Hūr here = 'light/splendor'), so the whole segment = 'thus, until the place of light became dark.'
segments.s220-g90: همی بود is a past-state clause: همی (still/continually) with بود (past of 'to be') gives 'there was/still was'. بر دشت is the locative 'on the plain'. هر گونه شور is the nominal phrase 'every kind/sort of tumult' serving as the predicate (what was present on the plain). Word order places the locative before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s220-g91: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. شد (became) is the past of شدن. دشت is the subject 'the plain'. بر گونه آبنوس is a simile 'in the fashion/appearance of ebony' (آبنوس used metaphorically as 'very dark/black'), so the segment reads 'when the plain became like ebony.'
segments.s220-g92: پراگنده گشتند is a compound predicate: پراگنده (scattered) + گشتند (they became/went), meaning 'they were scattered' or 'they dispersed'. کاموس و طوس are the plural subject 'Kāmos and Tūs' (proper names); here کاموس does not match the dictionary senses given, so its specific sense is unknown and marked -1. و is the coordinating 'and'.
segments.s220-g93: سوی خیمه (toward the tent) is the directional phrase placed before the verb. رفتند is past plural 'they went'. هر دو گروه ('both groups' or 'both companies') is the subject; its position after the verb is a normal poetic/inverted order for emphasis. The phrase means 'both companies went toward the tent.'
segments.s220-g94: This is a correlative pair: یکی ... و دگر ... meaning 'one ... and the other ...'. سوی دشت = 'toward the plain'; دگر سوی کوه = 'the other toward the mountain.' Word order is straightforward directional + target.
segments.s220-g95: چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'. گردون تهی شد ز خورشید و ماه = 'the heaven/sky became empty of sun and moon' — تهی شد is past passive/stative 'was emptied' or 'became empty', and ز before خورشید و ماه marks what it was emptied of ('of/from the sun and moon').
segments.s220-g96: طلایه برون شد ز هر دو سپاه = 'the vanguard went out from both armies.' طلایه (vanguard) is the subject; برون شد (went forth/came out) is the past action; ز هر دو سپاه ('from both armies') indicates origin. The verb شد here functions as the past 'came/appeared'.
segments.s220-g9: خروش آمد is a past-tense existential phrase 'a cry came/rose' (آمد = 'came'). بایران precedes the verb and here marks the side/interest affected (poetically 'for the Iranians' or 'on the Iranian side'); از دیده‌گاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the viewpoint/lookout' that gives the source. Word order is flexible and poetic: topical/affected phrase + predicate + source adjunct.
segments.s220-g10: کزین is a compact poetic form meaning 'from this' (کز + این) introducing the place of origin; روی تنگ = 'narrow face/front' (روی = side/face, تنگ = narrow). اندر آمد سپاه = 'the army came in/entered' with آمد (past) and سپاه as the agent/subject; اندر = 'into/in' marks direction/location. The literal order is 'from this narrow face into came the army' (poetic inversion).
segments.s220-g11: درفش سپهبد is a genitive/attributive grouping 'the banner of the spahbed' (noun + noun). گو here functions as the adjectival/epithet-forming element meaning 'so-called / called', attaching to the following name پیلتن ('Pilten'). This is a compact apposition: 'the spahbed's banner, called Pilten'.
segments.s220-g12: پدید آمد is a verb phrase 'appeared/was revealed' (پدید = appear, آمد = came/occurred). از دور is an adverbial 'from afar'; با انجمن 'with an assembly/retinue' modifies the manner of appearing. Order is verb first (poetic) followed by its source and company phrases.
segments.s220-g13: وزین روی دیگر is a compressed, archaic/poetic sequence equivalent to 'و ز این روی دیگر' — 'and from this other side'; وزین here functions as a fused conjunction+preposition ('and/from this'). ز توران سپاه explicitly names the origin and the actor: 'an army from Turan'. The line places the origin phrase before the noun 'سپاه' as topical information.
segments.s220-g14: هوا گشت = 'the air/atmosphere became' (گشت = became/turned). ابر سیاه is the predicate nominal 'a black cloud'. برسان here is a poetic connector meaning 'like/as' (cf. بسان) rather than its usual sense 'bring/deliver' — it links گشت to the simile 'a black cloud'. The overall sense: the atmosphere turned like a black cloud.
segments.s220-g15: سپهبد سواری = 'the spahbed (as) a rider/horseman' (noun apposition). چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile; یک لخت کوه = 'a single bare/naked mountain' (یک omitted in the provided words but understood). The phrase compares the mounted commander to a solitary, naked mountain — standard simile order: item being compared + چو + image.
segments.s220-g16: زمین گشته = 'the ground has become/was made' (گشته here as 'become/turned'); از نعل اسپش = 'by/through the horseshoe of his steed' (از marks cause/agent in a poetic causative sense, اسپش = 'his steed/horse'). ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary/ worn out'. The clause states the earth was made weary by the horse's iron shoe — participial/stateful result implied by گشته + adjective.
segments.s220-g369: بفرمود is an honorific/past third‑person verb 'he commanded/said'. The subordinate clause is introduced by تا (here 'so that/that'), followed by the past plural برکشیدند 'they raised' with its object کوس 'war‑drum' placed after the verb — a poetic word order where the verb comes before its object.
segments.s220-g370: بجنگ اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came into the fight' (بجنگ = 'into battle' + اندر 'in/into') with the past آمد 'came' as the main verb. سپهدار طوس follows the verb as the subject/agent ('the commander Tus'), a common poetic inversion where the subject comes after the verb.
segments.s220-g371: ازان کوه is an ablative/time/place phrase 'from that hill'. سر سوی هامون کشید is literal 'drew/turned (his) head toward the plain' with کشید as the final verb 'drew/turned/extended' and سر 'head' as the direct object of that motion; the verb‑final ordering is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s220-g372: همی is an adverbial particle (here giving immediacy or continuity). نیزه is the object 'spear'; از کینه is a causal phrase 'out of/because of hatred'; در خون کشید places the result/location 'into blood' with کشید the final verb meaning 'drove/ plunged/drew' (here transitive).
segments.s220-g373: بیک نیمه is an archaic fused phrase meaning 'for one half' (i.e. 'for half a day'); it is a temporal expression followed by از روز 'of the day'. لشکر is the subject 'the army' and گذشت is the past verb 'passed' placed at the end.
segments.s220-g374: کشیدند صف literally 'they drew up the line' (کشیدند = ‘they drew/formed’, صف = 'battle line/formation'), with the locative measure phrase بر دو فرسنگ دشت 'over two parasangs of plain' (بر + distance + دشت). The plural past verb precedes its object and locative phrase following poetic word order.
segments.s220-g375: ز گرد سپه is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/because of the dust of the army' (گرد = 'dust'). روشنایی نماند is an existential negative 'no brightness remained' with نماند 'remained' at the end as the main verb.
segments.s220-g376: ز خورشید sets the cause/source 'from the sun'; شب را uses را as the object marker ('the night') and جدایی نماند states 'no separation remained' — collectively meaning the sun's light was so strong that the night could not be separated from it. نماند is the past existential 'remained not'.
segments.s220-g329: یک امروز is an emphatic temporal phrase: یک here intensifies 'امروز' ('this very day' / 'one day, today'). در جنگ (literally 'in battle') is the locative phrase that the imperative verb کنيد governs; کنيد is a present/past-form imperative/polite plural of کردن used here to order or urge ('do/give' — 'give aid'). Word order is Topic (time/place) + object + verb, typical in Persian narratives.
segments.s220-g330: برین is an imperative-formal plural/archaic command meaning 'go upon/attack' (بر + -ین as an older second-person plural ending); it takes دشمنان ('the enemies') as its object. کامگاری کنید uses کامگاری ('success, fulfilment') with کنید (imperative 'do/bring about') to mean 'bring about success' or 'secure victory'. The two verbs are coordinated commands aimed at the addressees.
segments.s220-g331: که here functions as a subordinating conjunction meaning 'for/because' (giving the reason or assurance following the commands). گردان سپهر جهان is a noun phrase 'the host/troop of the heaven of the world' (گردان 'troop' modifies سپهر جهان 'heaven of the world'); یار ماست is the predicate 'is our ally' where ما + ست = 'is ours' (copula). Word order: relative/conjunction + noun phrase + predicate.
segments.s220-g332: مه و مهر is a paired nominal formula (here 'the Moon and [the Sun/Mithra]'); و is the coordinating conjunction. گردون نگهدار ماست is a nominal predicate: گردون ('the sky/heaven') + نگهدار ('guardian/preserver') + ماست ('is ours'). The whole clause states that these heavenly bodies are protectors. Note مهر is used poetically (Mithra / solar figure) rather than its common sense 'love'.
segments.s220-g333: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause functioning like English 'that' or 'so that' (reporting his order). طوس is the proper name 'Tūs' as the subject of the subordinate clause; بربست کوس is the predicate 'strike/set up the drum' (here بربست is a past action relating to sounding or setting the drum, with کوس 'drum' as object). The sequence shows reported command + reported action.
segments.s220-g334: بیاراست is a past narrative verb meaning 'he arrayed / set in order / made ready' (literally 'adorned'); لشکر is the direct object 'the army/host'. چو here means 'at the time of / when' (used adverbially), and چشم خروس ('the rooster's eye') is an idiomatic temporal image meaning 'cockcrow' or dawn. So the clause says he marshalled the army at cockcrow.
segments.s220-g335: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / commander.' بزد is the simple past third-person 'he struck/struck at' (often used for blowing or sounding instruments in epic). نای is the reed-flute (object). The coordinated phrase و رویینه خم attaches a second object: رویینه is an adjective 'iron/armored' modifying خم ('a curve' used here as the curved metal horn/trumpet). The verb governs both coordinated objects.
segments.s220-g336: خروش آمد is a simple verbal clause 'a roar arose' (خروش 'roar, tumult' + آمد past 'came/arose'). و links a second clause ناله گاودم: ناله 'lament/cry' qualified by گاودم ('cow's tail' / literally 'ox-tail' — here a poetic phrase describing a particular kind of wailing or the sound associated with an ox-tail instrument). Together they describe the sounds that followed the spahbed's sounding of instruments.
segments.s220-g153: بسی پندها داد و گفت is a short clause where بسی (archaic/poetic) modifies پندها to mean 'much/many advice'; پندها is the plural object and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. The sequence داد و گفت joins two past verbs 'gave and said' (a common narrative coordination). ای introduces a vocative, so ای سران is directly addressing the leaders after گفت.
segments.s220-g154: بپیش آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came forward/advanced' where بپیش is an adverbial/Directional prefix 'forward' and آمد is the past of 'to come'. امروز is a temporal adverb 'today' that may appear before or after the subject; رزمی گران is the subject (a compound meaning 'martial‑people' = fighters/warriors). The word order (adverb + verb + time + subject) is a poetic/flexible Persian order.
segments.s220-g155: چنین است is a fixed copular/introductory formula 'thus is' (or 'so it is'). آغاز و فرجام جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase 'the beginning and the outcome/end of war' where جنگ is the noun governed by the preceding nouns (genitive relation expressed by word order in poetry).
segments.s220-g156: یکی ... یکی is a parallel distributive structure 'one ... another ...'. تاج یابد uses یابد (present/factual or optative in classical style) meaning 'obtains/attains' (a general statement), so 'one attains a crown'. گور تنگ is a noun phrase 'a narrow grave' (تنگ qualifies گور). The verbs are omitted in the second clause (understood from the first), producing the antithesis.
segments.s220-g157: The clause places the object سراپرده ('inner curtain/inner chamber') before the verb زد (past of زدن 'to strike/pull/act on'), a permissible poetic inversion. گرد here functions adverbially 'around/about' or as a light particle marking motion/surrounding; گیتی‌فروز is the proper name (subject) placed after the verb — typical VSO flexibility in poetry.
segments.s220-g158: پس پشت او is an idiomatic locative phrase 'at his back/behind him' where پس conveys 'behind/after' and پشت 'back/rear'. لشکر نیمروز is a nominal phrase 'the army of Nimruz' (نیمروز as a place/name modifies لشکر). The clause therefore gives the scene: behind him stood the army of Nimruz.
segments.s220-g159: بکوه اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/within the mountain' (ب + کوه gives the spatial sense and اندرون 'inside' reinforces location). خیمه‌ها is the plural object 'tents' and ساختند is the past plural verb 'they built/erected'. The verb is plural, so the (implicit) subject is 'they.'
segments.s220-g160: درفش سپهبد برافراختند places the object 'درفش سپهبد' (the spahbed's banner/standard) before the past plural verb برافراختند 'they raised/hoisted'. In Persian poetry the genitive relation ('the banner of the spahbed') is often expressed without an explicit ezāfe, relying on word order and context.
segments.s220-g377: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and is used twice for rhythmic emphasis: 'ز تیر و ز پیکان' = 'from arrows and from darts'. هوا is the affected noun 'the air/sky' and تیره گشت is a verb phrase: تیره (adjective 'dark') + گشت (past of گشتن, 'became') — so literally 'the air became dark'. Word order (cause phrase — affected noun — verb) is typical in older/epic Persian.
segments.s220-g378: همی here is an emphatic/progressive particle (poetic), setting the action as ongoing or emphatic before the noun. آفتاب is the subject 'the sun'; اندرَان (archaic compound of اندر + ان) means 'therein/among them'. خیره گشت = خیره (adjective/participle 'astonished, stupefied') + گشت ('became') — so 'the sun there became astonished'.
segments.s220-g379: خروش is the noun 'clamor/uproar' and functions as the topic; سواران و اسپان ('horsemen and horses/steeds') specify whose clamor it is. ز + دشت ('from the plain') gives the origin. There is no finite verb in this segment; the action continues into the next segment where the verb appears.
segments.s220-g380: ز before proper names here indicates source/agency ('because of' or 'from' Bahram and Kiwan). بهرام and کیوان are coordinating names; in this martial/omenic context they are best read as planetary/poetic figures (Mars = Bahram, Saturn = Kiwan). همی is the same emphatic/progressive particle; برگذشت is the past of برگذشتن 'passed by/passed on' and is the verb that links back to the previous noun phrase (خروش).
segments.s220-g381: Again the preposition ز introduces causes: 'ز جوش سواران' ('from the surge/charge of the horsemen') and 'زخم تبر' ('the wound/blow of the axe'). جوش is used metaphorically as 'surge/charge' and تبر is the weapon 'battle-axe'. This clause sets causes or sources; the finite verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s220-g382: همی again adds emphatic/ongoing aspect. سنگ خارا is a compound 'flinty stones/rocks' (سنگ + خارا). برآورد is the past form of برآوردن/برآورد (in epic use meaning 'brought up, cast forth, sent aloft'); here it means 'sent/raised'. پر functions adverbially as 'full (with)' or 'into the air' — the whole phrase describes stones being hurled or filling the space.
segments.s220-g383: همه is the universal quantifier 'all'. تیغ و ساعد ('sword-blades and forearms') are the coordinated items; ز خون ('from blood' / 'with blood') gives the cause/coverage. بود is the past copula 'was/were' and لعل is the predicate noun/adjective 'ruby, crimson', so the clause reads 'all swords and forearms were ruby (with) blood.'
segments.s220-g384: خروشان is a present-participial/attributive form 'roaring' modifying دل خاک ('the heart of the earth' = earth's core/heart as a poetic image). در زیر نعل is a locative prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the horseshoe'. Together they create the image 'the earth's heart, roaring, beneath the horseshoe.' Word order places the vivid participle before the noun it modifies and the location phrase at the end.
segments.s220-g25: ز نزدیک is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning “from nearby”; it precedes the names it modifies. گودرز کشواد تفت is a sequence of names/titles in apposition (name(s) following one another without an explicit ezâfe, a common poetic pattern), so the whole phrase reads “Goudarz Keshvad Taft.”
segments.s220-g26: سواری here means “a rider/horseman.” بنزد is the prepositional/adpositional form “to/into the presence of” and is followed by the name فریبرز; رفت is a past-tense 3rd-pl./3rd-sg. form ‘went/rode’ placed at the end, giving “(the rider) went up to Fariborz.”
segments.s220-g27: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (“for/that”). توران سپه is the noun phrase “the Turanian host” (place-name + noun), سوی جنگ is a direction phrase “toward battle,” and آمدند is a past plural verb “they came,” placed at the end as normal in Persian.
segments.s220-g28: رده (rank/line) appears before the verb in typical poetic word order; برکشیدند is a past plural verb “they raised/drew up,” so رده برکشیدند = “they drew up their ranks.” و = “and.” تنگ here functions adverbially/adjectivally as “close/tight,” and آمدند (again past plural) completes the clause: “and they came close/closed in.”
segments.s220-g29: تو آن کن is an imperative construction: تو (you) + آن (that) + کن (do!), i.e. “do that.” The relative/correlative clause introduced by که explains what is to be done: از گوهر تو سزاست literally “it is deserved/from your essence,” where از = “from/of” and گوهر تو = “your worth/essence.” سزاست is a present/predicative phrase meaning “it is fitting/appropriate/what is deserved.”
segments.s220-g30: که here functions causally (“for/because”). تو مهتری is a nominal predicate: تو (you) + مهتری (the chief/preeminent one) — an older/poetic adjectival/noun predicate meaning “you are the foremost.” و = “and.” پدر پادشاست is another predicate: پدر (father) + پادشاست (is king/sovereign) with the copula fused into the predicate.
segments.s220-g31: که again begins an explanatory clause. گرد before the proper name تهمتن denotes a band/group/host in this poetic context (appositional/collective use), followed by the past intransitive verb برآمد “arose/emerged.” ز راه is an ablative phrase “from the road/way,” indicating origin of movement.
segments.s220-g32: هم‌اکنون is an adverb “right now/at once.” بیاید is a jussive/subjunctive form meaning “may come / will come” (here giving impending arrival). بدین رزمگاه is the destination ‘to this battlefield’ (ب = “to/into”; دین in the segmentation stands for the demonstrative element “this” in the fused form بدین, so together with رزمگاه it means “to this battlefield”).
segments.s220-g297: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing cause). بانگ is a noun 'cry/clamor' and here heads the causal phrase; it is followed by تبیره which is unusual in meaning in this line (see senses). The phrase ز بانگ تبیره therefore means 'from/at the (resounding/terrible) cry'.
segments.s220-g298: بپوشید is a past tense verb (with the poetic prefix بـ) here meaning 'covered' (پوشیدن → to cover). In classic Persian the verb can appear before its subject; کوه is the subject 'the mountain'. و links the two verb phrases; بیفگند (past of افکندن with prefixed بی-) means 'cast/throw down', and مهر is used here as a proper-name/deity/sun (Mehr).
segments.s220-g299: بفرمود is an honorific past tense 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that'. مهد is 'cradle' and بر پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase 'on the elephant's back' (بر = on, پشت = back, پیل = elephant). The whole segment is 'He ordered that they fasten the cradle on the elephant's back.'
segments.s220-g300: ببستند is the past plural 'they bound / they tied' (verb before subject omitted but implied). و is the coordinating 'and/then'. شد is past 'became'. روی گیتی is a nominal phrase 'the face/surface of the world' (روی = face, گیتی = world). چو نیل is a simile 'like the Nile' (چو = like).
segments.s220-g301: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came'. گرازان is the plural subject 'boars' (the verb precedes the subject, a common epic/order in classical Persian). بقلب سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'into the heart/center of the army' (بـ = into, قلب = heart/center, سپاه = host/army).
segments.s220-g302: شد is the past 'became'. از گرد here uses گرد in its spatial/relational sense 'around' (از گرد X = 'from/about X' or 'around X'), so از گرد خورشید تابان qualifies the resulting state; خورشید تابان is 'the shining sun' (noun + adjective), and سیاه is the adjective 'black/dark' describing the subject (the boars/the scene) that 'became' so.
segments.s220-g303: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'to roar' or 'the roaring' and here introduces the sound. زنگ is 'bell/ ringing' and و connects it to هندی 'Indian' (here an adjective identifying the (Indian) instruments). درای in the word list is given as 'possessing/wealthy' but that meaning doesn't fit this phrase; it likely names an instrument or quality in the poem and is unclear here (marked accordingly).
segments.s220-g304: همی is the classical progressive/intensifying particle used before verbs (here giving a sense of immediate or continued action). دل is 'heart'. برآورد is a verb meaning 'brought up' / 'raised' (here 'stirred up' or 'lifted the heart'); the exact dictionary senses given do not perfectly match but the usage is 'to cause to rise'. گفتی is used here as 'you said/spoke' (in context of the preceding lines); ز جای is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the place/from his place'.
segments.s220-g129: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that/for' introducing the statement. گیتی 'world' is the subject; سراسر 'entirely' is an adverb modifying the predicate. فریبست is فریب + ست (copula) = 'is deception/is deceptive'. The conjunction و links بند 'a bond/snare' as a second predicate; the copula is expressed only once (ellipsis), so فریبست governs both فریب and بند.
segments.s220-g130: گهی ... گاهی is a paired adverbial pattern meaning 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. سودمندی is an abstract noun 'usefulness/benefit' and گزند 'harm'. The coordinating و simply connects the two alternatives.
segments.s220-g131: The pattern یکی را ... یکی را ... lists contrasting destinies: یکی 'some/one' + را (definite/object marker) followed by a prepositional verb phrase. ببستر here functions as a contracted form of به بستر 'to the bed' (i.e. 'to be laid on a bed' / 'kept to the bed'); بجنگ likewise = به جنگ 'to battle'. The finite verb that governs these objects is omitted (elliptical), so each phrase implies 'are given/are sent' or 'fall to'.
segments.s220-g132: Same parallel structure: یکی را بنام = یکی را به نام 'to (be) named/to (be) given renown' and یکی را بننگ = یکی را به ننگ 'to disgrace/shame'. و simply coordinates the last two items. را again marks the definite object/component whose verb is omitted.
segments.s220-g133: همی رفت باید is a classical order where همی (an iterative/emphatic particle 'still/continually') + رفت 'goes' and the modal باید 'must' yield 'it must continue' or 'it must be so'. کزین = ک + زین ('that/from this') introduces the reason, and چاره نیست = 'there is no remedy/solution' (نیست negates existence).
segments.s220-g134: مرا نیز = 'for me also' (مرا object + نیز 'also'). از مرگ = 'from death' (از introducing source/cause). پتیاره is an obsolete/archaic noun meaning roughly 'deliverance/escape' (contextually 'deliverance from death'); نیست negates it: 'I, too, have no deliverance from death.' Note: the tokenization provided splits مرا into separate items in the word list, but syntactically this segment reads as مرا نیز از مرگ پتیاره نیست.
segments.s220-g135: روان تو = 'your soul/spirit' (possessive order). از درد 'from pain' marks what the soul is freed of, and بی‌درد 'without pain' is an adjectival phrase. باد at the end is the optative particle 'may' (a common classical Persian wish form): 'May your spirit be free of pain.'
segments.s220-g136: This clause is an optative wish with باد again marking 'may'. همه رفتن ما = 'all our going(s)' (رفتن here is a verbal noun 'going/departure' and can carry the nuance of journey or of passing/death). بآورد is the present subjunctive of آورد 'bring/cause' and with باد expresses hope: 'may it bring/put an end to all our goings' (the line is somewhat idiomatic and ambiguous: it can mean 'may it remove all that causes our going' or 'may all our departures be brought/ended').
segments.s220-g177: ز پیلان is the prepositional phrase 'from the elephants' (poetic 'ز' = 'از'). جنگی here functions adverbially/attributively 'in battle' or 'warlike', and گریز ندارد is the negated nominal predicate 'has no flight/escape' (دارد = 'has', negated by ندارد). Word order is poetic: the adverbial/qualifier (جنگی) and the negated predicate surround the noun گریز 'escape'.
segments.s220-g178: سرش 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive ش). پر ز + noun is the idiom 'full of X' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from'), so سرش پر ز کینست = 'his head is full of hatred' where کینست is the copulative phrase 'is hatred/hatred it is' (poetic fused form). و links the two coordinated predicates; دل پر ستیز = 'his heart full of strife' with ستیز used as noun 'strife'. Note the adjective-like پر follows the possessed noun and takes the 'ز' + noun complement.
segments.s220-g179: ازین is the poetic concatenation of 'از این' ('from this'); کوه is its object: 'from this mountain'. تا پیش introduces the extent 'up to, as far as in front of', and دریای شهد is a genitive phrase 'the sea of honey' (دریا + genitiveِ شهد). The whole segment is an adverbial range setting the spatial limits for the following nominal list.
segments.s220-g180: This is a poetic nominal clause listing what is present: درفش و سپاهست و پیلان و مهد. سپاهست is the compound of سپاه + است (here fused as سپاهست) functioning as the verb 'there is / a host is' in the phrase; درفش is linked to that copula by و. The remaining nouns (پیلان, مهد) are added by و — a simple inventory-of-things construction. The copula is compressed into one item (سپاهست) to serve the predicate for the whole list.
segments.s220-g181: "اگر سوی ما پهلوان سپاه" is a conditional front-clause: اگر introduces the 'if' condition; سوی ما (literally 'toward us') marks the beneficiary/target of the action; پهلوان سپاه is a poetic juxtaposition meaning 'a champion of the host/army' (the ezafe is omitted in verse). Word order is topical/poetic (condition first, then target and agent noun phrase).
segments.s220-g182: نکردی is the 2nd-person singular past negative of کردن ('you did not do / you did not cause'). گذر کار is a compound noun phrase 'گذرِ کار' meaning 'the course/passage of the affair' (course of events). گشتی is the past 2nd-person form of گشتن used here in the sense 'became/turned' (poetic usage), and تباه is the predicative adjective 'ruined/spoiled'. Together they state the result clause: '... the course of the affair became ruined.'
segments.s220-g183: سپاس از + noun is a fixed formula meaning 'thanks to' or 'praise be to.' از governs the following noun phrase, here خداوند پیروزگر, 'the Lord (God), the victorious/triumphant one.' Word order is noun (سپاس) + prepositional phrase indicating the addressee/recipient of the praise.
segments.s220-g184: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he' (referring to the Lord). آورد is the past tense of آوردن 'brought/caused', here transitive. رنج و سختی is a coordinated object 'suffering and hardship.' بسر follows annab idiomatically to indicate 'to be endured / upon (one's) lot' — together the clause means 'who brought suffering and hardship to be borne.'
segments.s220-g281: سپاه is the grammatical subject ('the army'). The sequence از تو دارد همی is a slightly inverted, poetic order: دارد (present-tense 'has/keeps') is the verb, همی is a habitual/continuous particle marking an ongoing or customary action, and از تو here marks the person toward/with whom that disposition exists. پشت راست is a noun + adjective ('rear/behind' + 'straight/turned') describing what the army 'keeps.' Word order (Subject — [prep phrase] — Verb — complement) is somewhat flexible in poetry.
segments.s220-g282: چنان کن is an imperative phrase ('do so / act thus'), with کن the imperative of کردن. That-clause introduced by که gives the reason or standard: از گوهر تو سزاست = 'it is fitting/deserved from/of your essence' — از + گوهر تو = 'of/according to your essence/ worth' and سزاست is a nominal copular construction meaning 'it is due/fitting.' The relative conjunction که here links the imperative to its explanatory clause.
segments.s220-g283: This short clause is an imperative instruction whose main verb form is poetic/archaic. بیارای here functions as an instruction 'adorn/prepare' (poetic imperative), followed by پیلان (elephants) as its object. بزنگ و درای are instruments or attributes joined by و ('and'), describing what the elephants should bear (bells, drums). The ordering (verb — object — instrument phrases) is common in epic diction.
segments.s220-g284: جهان پر کن is another imperative: جهان = 'the world', پر = 'full', and کن (imperative of کردن) means 'make/fill.' از introduces the source/contents that will fill the world (از ناله کرنای = 'with the lament of the trumpet'), with ناله (lament/moan) modified by کرنای (of the trumpet). The whole is an imperative request to 'fill the world with the trumpet's wailing.'
segments.s220-g285: من امروز جنگ آورم is a present/habitual or immediate-future assertion: من 'I', امروز 'today', جنگ آورم is a verbal phrase literally 'I bring war' (آورم = 1st-person present of آوردن), meaning 'I wage/lead the battle.' با سپاه attaches the complement 'against/with your army' — با here marks accompaniment/force.
