segments.s220-g286: This is a prepositional/adverbial phrase qualifying how/with whom the speaker fights: تو (vocative/subject) + با پیل و با کوس lists the forces 'with elephant and with drum' (با repeated for emphasis/parallelism). در قلبگاه is a locative phrase 'in/at the heart/core' specifying the central place of action. The conjunctions and repeated با are typical poetic coordination.
segments.s220-g287: نگه‌دار is a vocative or imperative form meaning 'keeper/hold fast' or 'be guardian'; پشت سپاه is the noun phrase 'the rear/behind of the army' (پشت = rear, سپاه = army). مرا is the clitic object 'for me / my' (here split in the manuscript listing), so the whole is a request/order meaning 'hold the army's rear for me' or 'be the army's rear for me.' The syntax places the guardian role first then the object phrase.
segments.s220-g288: This sequence contains two imperatives/commands in succession: بابر is a proper name called in vocative (or the person ordered to act), اندر آور is a compound verb 'bring in / lead inside' (اندر = 'into/within', آور = 'bring'), and کلاه مرا is the direct object 'my helmet' (کلاه = helmet/hat, مرا = 'my' as object/clitic). The word-order Verb — Object is common in commands; را is the definite/object marker attached to reinforce the object.
segments.s220-g145: سپاه و سپهبد پیاده شدند is a coordinated subject followed by a past copula. Two nouns (سپاه and سپهبد) are joined by و and share the predicate پیاده شدند. پیاده (an adjectival/participial predicate meaning 'on foot' or 'dismounted') comes before شدند, the past plural of شدن, giving 'they dismounted'. Word order is subject(s) → predicate.
segments.s220-g146: میان بسته و دلگشاده شدند contains two coordinated predicate phrases that share the same subject as the previous clause (ellipsis of the subject is normal). میان بسته is a noun (میان, 'middle') + adjective (بسته, 'closed') meaning 'the middle was closed' or 'they closed the center/ranks'. دلگشاده شدند is an adjectival predicate (دلگشاده, 'heart‑at‑ease/open‑hearted') + copula شدند; و links the two predicates.
segments.s220-g147: خروشی برآمد ز لشکر بدرد is built as noun + verb + prepositional phrase. خروشی (a substantive, 'a cry/roar') precedes the verb برآمد ('arose/issued forth'). ز لشکر ('from the host/army') is a prepositional phrase placed after the verb (poetic word order). بدرد is an idiomatic predicate ('to be of use/avail'); here it functions as the gist of the exclamation (i.e. 'it will not help' or 'it is of no avail').
segments.s220-g148: ازان کشتگان زیر خاک نبرد is a noun phrase that supplies the content of the cry. ازان ('of those/from that') introduces reference to 'those' (the slain). کشتگان is the plural participial noun 'the slain'. زیر خاک is a locative phrase 'under the earth/soil' and نبرد ('battle') postmodifies خاک (under the earth/dust of battle); Persian often stacks these nouns without an explicit ezāfe in older/poetic usage.
segments.s220-g149: دل رستم از درد ایشان بخست places the possessor + noun (دل رستم, 'Rostam's heart') before an intervening prepositional/causal phrase از درد ایشان ('from/because of their pain'), then the past verb بخست (archaic past of بستن, 'shut/contracted'). The past-tense verbal form is older/poetic; the construction expresses an emotional reaction ('his heart contracted/was closed because of their pain').
segments.s220-g150: بکینه بنوی میان را ببست consists of an adverbial/prepositional phrase بکینه ('with hatred / in revenge') modifying the main verb ببست ('he shut/closed', past third person). میان را is the definite object ('the middle/center') marked by the object marker را. بنوی is an archaic/poetic item here sitting between the adverbial and the object; in classical usage بنوی can be related to 'نوشتن' or function as a light/parenthetical verbal element, but the main syntactic action is ببست ('he closed'), so read the clause as 'in hatred he closed the middle.'
segments.s220-g151: بنالید ازان پس بدرد سپاه begins with the past verb بنالید ('he moaned/wailed'). ازان پس ('after that') is a temporal phrase following the verb (poetic inversion is common). بدرد سپاه here indicates the object/causal domain of the lament—literally 'for/over the army's pain'—with بدرد functioning idiomatically to point to the suffering and سپاه as the affected party.
segments.s220-g152: چو آگه شد از کار آوردگاه is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو ('when'). آگه شد is the two-word predicate 'became aware' (آگه 'aware' + شد 'became', past). از کار آوردگاه is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of the affairs/state of the battlefield' (کار 'affair/state' + آوردگاه 'battlefield'). Together: 'when he learned of what had happened on the battlefield.'
segments.s220-g161: نشست is a past tense (3rd person) verb here meaning “sat/took a seat.” از بر تخت is a poetic locative phrase “from off the throne” (از + بر + تخت), and بر پیلتن means “upon Pīltan” (بر + place name). Word order stacks the prepositional phrases, so the clause reads literally “sat from the throne upon Pīltan.”
segments.s220-g162: بزرگان لشکر is a genitive/compound noun phrase “the great ones/of the army.” شدند is the past plural of شدن meaning “became” or here idiomatically “assembled.” انجمن is the predicate noun “assembly,” so the clause means “the army’s nobles became (formed) an assembly” = “the great men assembled.”
segments.s220-g163: ز یک دست is an idiomatic poetic expression “on one side” (literally “from one hand”). بنشست is 3rd-person past “sat/took their seat.” The proper names گودرز و گیو follow the phrase as the subjects: “Goudarz and Giv sat on one side.”
segments.s220-g164: بدست دگر functions as “on the other side/hand” (بدست + دگر). طوس و گردان نیو names the people on that side: طوس (Tūs) and گردان (a troop/unit) headed by نیو (here a proper name or group name). The clause omits an explicit verb because it parallels the previous clause (they sat).
segments.s220-g165: فروزان is an adjective “radiant/shining” qualifying the indefinite numeric phrase یکی شمع (“one/a single candle”). بنهاد is 3rd-person past “placed/put,” and پیش here means “before/in front (of them).” The phrase literally reads “a shining one placed one candle before (them).”
segments.s220-g166: سخن رفت is an idiomatic, somewhat impersonal phrasing meaning “speech proceeded / words were spoken.” هر گونه modifies the manner (“in every kind/way”), and بر کم و بیش means “with less or more” (i.e. “more or less”). The clause thus says that talk proceeded in various ways, to greater or lesser extent.
segments.s220-g167: ز introduces topics (“about/concerning”). کار بزرگان is “the deeds/affairs of the great ones,” and جنگ سپاه is “the battles/war of the host/army.” The two noun phrases are coordinated topics of discussion introduced by ز.
segments.s220-g168: The second parallel phrase also begins with ز (“concerning”) and lists poetic epithets: رخشنده خورشید is “the radiant sun” and گردنده ماه is “the turning/revolving moon.” These are adjectival modifiers preceding the nouns in classical poetic order.
segments.s220-g233: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' marking material or source; دیبا is the material noun 'brocade' governed by ز. یکی is the indefinite numeral 'one' introducing a single item; سبز is an adjective ('green') qualifying the following noun پرده‌سرای. Word order is compact and poetic (ز + material + one + adjective + noun) rather than colloquial Persian order.
segments.s220-g234: یکی again marks 'one' (another single object). اژدهافش is a compound adjective 'dragon‑emblazoned/dragon-bearing' modifying درفشی, which names the banner/standard; the phrase is compact: 'one dragon‑faced banner'. بپای is an archaic/poetic verbal form from پاییدن (here intransitive) meaning 'it stood/abided/was stationed' — the prefix ب- is the old prepositional/verb prefix fused to the verb in poetry.
segments.s220-g235: سپاهی is a noun 'soldier/warrior'. بگرد here is a compact poetic verbal form expressing motion or circling (the literal imperative form is listed in the lexicon, but in context it functions descriptively: 'moving/turning about' or 'was around'). اندر + ش (an enclitic) = 'in it' (اندر = 'in/within', -ش = 'it/his'), and زابلی identifies origin 'from Zābul' — the whole unit describes a warrior positioned in/around the described object.
segments.s220-g236: سپردار is a noun 'commander' linked to the previous noun phrase; و simply coordinates/adds another descriptive phrase. با introduces an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with' and خنجر کابلی is a noun + adjective 'a Kabul dagger' (خنجر = 'dagger', کابلی = 'of Kabul/Kabulite'). The construction lists attendant persons/items by coordination.
segments.s220-g237: گمانم is the speaker's comment 'I suppose/I think' formed from the noun گمان plus the first‑person suffix -م. که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause. رستم is the subject, and ز + نزدیک شاه literally 'from near the king' (ز = 'from/of', نزدیک شاه = 'near the king') — in context it conveys Rostam's origin or proximity relative to the king.
segments.s220-g238: بیاری here functions as an instrumental/associative noun 'with help/with companions' placed before the verb; بیامد is the past tense 'came'. The short poetic sequence ب + دین + رزمگاه corresponds to بن/به این رزمگاه ('to/into this battlefield') with ب = 'to' and رزمگاه = 'battlefield' — دین is a dialectal/poetic form standing for 'این' (this) and is left as a fossilized element in the line.
segments.s220-g239: بدو is the prepositional clitic + pronoun 'to him/to it' (i.e., 'said to him'). گفت here is the narrative verb 'said' (the lexicon entry lists noun senses but in the poem it functions as the past verb 'he said'); پیران is the speaker's name or title (here used as the person speaking). که introduces the content clause 'that'; بد روزگار is an idiomatic adjective + noun 'ill‑fated / bad time' describing the state of affairs.
segments.s220-g240: اگر is the conditional 'if' that opens the protasis. رستم is the subject; آید is the present/subjunctive form 'comes' (used in conditional clauses to mean 'should/if he comes'); ب + دین + کارزار corresponds to بن/به این کارزار 'to/into this battle' (again دین = poetic for 'این'). The clause sets up a hypothetical consequence introduced in the following lines.
segments.s220-g217: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (classical variant of از) indicating material or composition; پیروزه (adjective) precedes دیبا (noun) to form 'turquoise brocade'; سراپرده is the direct object 'inner curtain/inner tent'; دید is the simple past (3rd singular) 'he saw' placed at the end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s220-g218: فراوان ('abundant/many') modifies what follows; بگرد here is a compressed poetic form related to 'گردیدن' and functions as a participial/descrptive element meaning something like 'turning about / surrounding' (poetic usage); اندر + ش is a locative 'within it' (ش is the enclitic 3rd‑person possessive 'its/his'); پرده is 'curtain', and the final دید is again the past tense 'he saw'. The clause therefore describes that he saw many curtains about/within it.
segments.s220-g219: This is a coordination: درفش 'banner' و 'and' سنان 'spear' form a noun phrase; سپهبد (spahbed) is a title that follows, and بپیش is an adverbial/locative meaning 'in front / before (someone)'. The construction lists items and places them 'in front'.
segments.s220-g220: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; گردش (literally 'turning' or 'course') here combines with اختر 'star' in a genitive relation (the star's turning) to mean the course/fate determined by the star; بد is an adjective 'bad / unfavorable' describing that turning; بپیش again places it 'before/ahead'. The clause lacks an explicit copula, so read as 'the same ill‑fated turning of the star lay before him.'
segments.s220-g221: سراپرده‌ای is the indefinite form of 'سراپرده' (the -ای ending makes it 'an inner curtain/inner tent'); دید is past 'he saw'; دیگر 'another' and سیاه 'black' are adjectival modifiers (classical Persian freely places adjectives after the noun).
segments.s220-g222: درفشی here denotes something pertaining to a banner or a standard; درفشان is a participial/epithet 'glittering, flashing' (literally 'banner‑shining'); بکردار is a poetic compound that introduces manner/appearance ('in the form/shape of' in context) and is followed by ماه 'moon', so the phrase describes a banner glittering and having a moonlike appearance. (Note: بکردار is a poetic/archaic formation used to indicate appearance/character here.)
segments.s220-g223: فریبرز and کاوس are proper names presented in apposition (common in epic lists); با 'with' introduces the instruments/companions 'پیل' (elephants) و 'and' 'کوس' (war‑drum or signal). The whole phrase sets up who is acting and with what accompaniment.
segments.s220-g224: فراوان ('many' / 'much') modifies the verb/participial زده ('struck' or 'having struck'); here زده is a past participle/past verb and with فراوان indicates that many blows/signals were struck (i.e., many drums were beaten); خیمه 'tent' with نزدیک 'near' and طوس 'Tus' form the locative phrase 'the tent (was) near Tus' — subject and auxiliary are omitted as is typical in Persian narrative, so read as 'he struck many (drums) and pitched the tent near Tus.'
segments.s220-g33: فریبرز is the proper name (subject). با + لشکری is a prepositional phrase 'with an army/force' where لشکری functions as the noun 'troops, soldiery'. گرد نیو is a genitive/attributive phrase modifying لشکری ('the contingent/band of Niu'); گرد here is part of that nominal group and the exact nuance is dialectal/poetic, so the whole phrase reads 'a contingent of Niu'. Word order is nominative phrase → prepositional instrument → modifying noun.
segments.s220-g34: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he/they came/arrived.' بپیوست is a past 3rd‑person singular/plural form meaning 'he/they joined' (here continuing the narrative: having arrived, he joined). با طوس و گیو is the prepositional complement 'with Tous and Giv' (طوس and گیو are proper names); the sequence of two verbs (بیامد بپیوست) is a simple narrative coordination 'came and joined.'
segments.s220-g35: بر کوه is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the mountain' (بر = on/upon, کوه = mountain). لشکر is the direct object/subject being acted on ('the army/host'), and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they arrayed/they set in order' (بیاراستند = they adorned/arrayed). Word order here places the locative first for emphasis: 'on the mountain they arrayed the army.'
segments.s220-g36: درفش خجسته is a noun phrase 'the auspicious banner/standard' (درفش = banner, خجسته = auspicious/blessed). بپیراستند is another past plural verb 'they adorned/they set (it) up' and links back to the same subject as the previous verb: they arrayed the army and (they) adorned/raised the good banner. The b‑ prefix marks classical/poetic verb formation.
segments.s220-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' با is the preposition 'with/by.' میسره is a unit/name (here likely a division or technical term in the battle array) and is the object of با (its exact historical/technical sense is dialectal or specialized). راست شد is a compound predication: راست (adjective 'straight/right') + شد (past 'became/was made') = 'was made straight' or 'was aligned.' میمنه ('the right side/right flank') is the noun that the verb applies to; word order is slightly inverted (predicate then noun) for poetic effect: 'when by X the right flank was made straight.'
segments.s220-g38: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'the same' or 'that very.' ساقه و قلب و جای is a coordinated list of nouns (shaft/pole, heart/boss or core, and place/base) — likely parts of the standard/flag. بنه here follows the list and functions as a verb 'they set/placed' or as a nominal indicating the base; the exact historical sense is obscure in this fragment, so the pragmatic reading is 'they put/placed the same shaft, boss, and base.' The nouns are listed in the usual Persian order with و 'and' linking them.
segments.s220-g39: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth.' خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'roaring' used here substantively ('a roar'). کرنای is the genitive 'of the trumpet/horn' (کرنا = horn/trumpet). The construction literally reads 'A roaring of the trumpet arose' — a poetic way to say the trumpets blared.
segments.s220-g40: سپه is the subject 'the army/host.' چون سپهر is a simile 'like the sky/heaven' (چون = like, سپهر = sky) comparing the army's appearance or vastness to the heavens. اندر آمد ز جای is a verbal phrase 'came forth from (their) place/position' (اندر آمد = came in/appeared, ز = from, جای = place/position). The whole clause means 'the army, like the sky, came forth from its station.'
segments.s220-g241: This is a paired negative construction: 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'کاموس ماند' is a verbless clause in which the verb 'ماند' (past 3rd sg of ماندن) provides the predicate for the first noun; the second negative clause repeats 'نه' but omits the verb (ellipsis), so the same verb is understood for both. 'خاقان چین' is a noun phrase in which 'خاقان' (khaghan, sovereign) is qualified by 'چین' (China) placed after it, the classical Persian order for a title + place.
segments.s220-g242: Continuation of the neither–nor series: another 'نه' introduces 'شنگل' (an obscure name/term) and then 'نه گردان توران زمین'. 'گردان' here functions as 'troop/host' and is followed by the genitival phrase 'توران زمین' = 'the land of Turan' (land/place after the noun). The verb 'ماند' from the first clause is understood to govern these subjects as well (ellipsis across the coordinated negatives).
segments.s220-g243: 'هم‌انگه' is a temporal adverb ('at that moment'); 'ز لشکر' is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (preposition ز before the noun). 'گه اندر کشید' contains 'گه' (occasion/moment) + 'اندر' (in/into) and the verb 'کشید' (past 3rd sg). In classical narrative 'کشید' (from کشیدن) often means 'drew/moved' — here it denotes a swift movement drawn out of the army (the verb is past and applies to the preceding temporal phrase).
segments.s220-g244: Two verbs in sequence: 'بیامد' (past 3rd sg 'he came') followed by 'سپهدار را بنگرید'. 'سپهدار را' is marked with the object marker 'را', and 'بنگرید' is an archaic/polite past-form (3rd sg) of 'نگریستن' meaning 'he looked (upon)'. Word order places the object between the two verbs; logically the second verb takes 'سپهدار را' as its object.
segments.s220-g245: 'وزانجا' = 'and/from there' is a connective place adverb. 'دمان' (دامن) here means 'skirt, outskirts, border' and is the goal of motion; 'سوی' marks direction 'toward'. 'کاموس' is the place or person toward whom motion is directed. 'شد' is the past 3rd sg auxiliary/lexical verb 'became/went/turned' indicating the change of direction or arrival.
segments.s220-g246: 'بنزدیک' is an adverb/adpositional phrase meaning 'near' or 'close to'. It is followed by two coordinated nouns 'منشور و فرطوس' (names or items) joined by 'و'. The clause closes with 'شد' (past 3rd sg) indicating that he came to or became close to those persons/places. The verb governs the whole coordinated noun phrase as its complement.
segments.s220-g247: 'که' introduces a subordinate (explanatory) clause here. 'شبگیر' is a temporal adverb/adjective 'during the night' or 'night-time'. 'ز ایدر برفتم' = 'I departed from here' where 'ز' = 'from' + 'ایدر' = 'here' and 'برفتم' is the past 1st sg (classical prefix بر- plus رفتن) 'I went/left'. 'پگاه' ('dawn') is placed after this clause to set the next temporal point (it links to the following verb).
segments.s220-g248: 'بگشتم' is an old/pedantic past form meaning 'I returned' or 'I turned back' (1st person past). 'همه گرد ایران سپاه' is a noun phrase: 'همه' = 'all; the whole', 'گرد' = 'around/about' used adverbially with the following genitival phrase 'ایران سپاه' = 'the army of Iran' so the phrase describes the entire Iranian host gathered around. Word order places the adverbial/quantifier before the head noun and the whole phrase complements the return-action by describing the assembled force.
segments.s220-g17: A noun phrase in which the numeral/indefinite 'یکی' precedes the noun 'گرز' (standard SOV/NP ordering in epic Persian for emphasis). 'همچون' is the simile marker 'like' and introduces the comparison 'سر گاومیش' (the head of a buffalo). Together it reads 'a mace like the head of a buffalo'; the phrase is nominal and stands as a scene-setting image.
segments.s220-g18: A verbless / elliptical clause describing troop disposition: 'سپاه از پس' = 'the army (was) at the rear/behind' where 'از پس' (lit. 'from after') marks location. The conjunction 'و' links the next coordinate 'نیزه‌دارانش پیش' = 'his spearmen (were) in front'; the possessive suffix on 'نیزه‌دارانش' identifies whose spearmen. The copula 'was' is omitted as typical in poetic narrative.
segments.s220-g19: 'همی' plus present-tense-form 'جوشد' is a poetic/habitual present construction meaning 'keeps gushing' or 'ever gushes.' 'از گرز' gives the source 'from the mace.' The demonstrative 'آن' qualifies the two nouns 'یال و کفت' ('that mane and froth'), which are the things gushing; 'کفت' here means 'froth/foam.'
segments.s220-g20: 'سزد‌گر' is an impersonal/predicative form meaning 'fitting/appropriate' (literally 'it is fitting'); it introduces the evaluative clause that follows. 'بمانی' is an optative/ subjunctive 2nd-person singular 'you should/remain' (i.e. 'that you remain'), 'ازو' = 'about/from it', 'در شگفت' = 'in/with wonder.' The whole line: 'it would be fitting that you remain amazed by it.'
segments.s220-g21: 'وزین روی' is an adjective+noun ('dignified/weighty face'), with 'ایران' in genitive relation: 'the dignified face of Iran.' 'سپهدار طوس' is an appositive title + name 'the commander Tūs' and forms the subject phrase that will perform the action in the next segment. Poetic word order places descriptive phrases together before the verb.
segments.s220-g22: 'اندر آورد' is the past-tense verbal periphrasis 'brought in/brought forth' (آورد = brought). 'بابر' here appears as a proper name/epithet presented with the subject phrase (poetic apposition) and 'آوای کوس' is the direct object 'the sound/voice of the war‑drum.' The verb thus asserts that the (named) commander produced the drum-call.
segments.s220-g23: 'خروشیدن' is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring/outcry' placed before its agent; 'دیده‌بان پهلوان' ('the sentinel, the champion') follows as the agent/possessor, yielding 'the roar of the champion sentinel' or 'the champion sentinel's shout.' This is a typical genitive/agent construction in epic diction.
segments.s220-g24: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'; 'بشنید' is a simple past 'he heard.' 'شد' is the past auxiliary 'became,' linking to the predicate adjectives 'شاد و روشن‌روان' ('happy and bright‑soul(ed)' = cheerful, light‑spirited). Word order is subject+verb+resulting adjectives, with 'و' coordinating the two qualities.
segments.s220-g57: چو introduces a temporal clause here (= “when”). نزدیک‌تر شد is a past-tense periphrastic idea: شد (“became”) with the comparative نزدیک‌تر = “grew nearer / approached.” بکاموس گفت is the main-clause reporting verb (“Bākāmūs said”); in Persian the temporal clause (چو ... شد) precedes the main clause.
segments.s220-g58: که is the complementizer introducing reported speech (= “that” / here: the content of what he said). این را uses را as the definite/object marker, pointing to ‘this (one).’ مگر here is the rhetorical/epistemic use (= “perhaps / is it not?”). ژنده is an adjective/noun meaning “rag, ragged,” پیلست is an archaic/obscure lexeme (here functioning as ‘elephant’ or a word built on 'pil' = elephant), and جفت is the predicate noun “pair.” Note Persian word order: the clause ends with the noun predicate (جفت).
segments.s220-g59: کمان برکشید is a past action: کمان (object) comes before the verb برکشید (“drew up / drew the bow” — here transitive). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بزه بر نهاد: نهاد is past of نهادن (“placed”), with بر + noun indicating placement on/onto (بزه = the arrow/shaft here); object-marking را is omitted but understood.
segments.s220-g60: ز is the poetic preposition = از (“from / of”). دادار is the epithet for the Creator/God (the giver). نیکی دهش is a noun phrase: نیکی = “goodness,” دهش = “gift / granting” (i.e. “the gift of goodness”). کرد is the light verb “did / made,” and یاد (“remembrance”) is the thing he did — i.e. “he remembered” or “he recalled.” Word order is topical (z-phrase + possessor + noun) followed by the light verb.
segments.s220-g61: بکاموس is the subject (proper name). بر + تیرباران forms an adverbial/idiomatic phrase meaning “into/with a rain/shower of arrows” (تیرباران = volley/shower of arrows). گرفت is the past-tense verb (“took” / idiomatically “began” or “made” — here ‘let loose’ or ‘began to rain arrows’). The verb follows the adverbial phrase, typical SOV ordering.
segments.s220-g62: کمان را: کمان is the definite object, marked by را. چو = “like / as” introduces a simile. ابر بهاران = “spring cloud(s)” (ابر = cloud, بهاران = of spring / springtime); گرفت is repeated past verb “took / seized / grasped.” The clause literally says he took the bow “like a spring cloud,” i.e. with the sweep/force of gathering spring clouds — a poetic simile. Note object before verb and simile introduced by چو.
segments.s220-g63: چو = “when.” کاموس (here a proper name) is the subject. دست و گشادش is a coordinated noun phrase: دست = “hand,” و = “and,” گشادش = گشاد + possessive suffix “-sh” = “its/his looseness/slackness” (i.e. a loose grip). بدید is the past form of دیدن = “saw.” Together: “When Kāmos saw his hand and its slackness/looseness...”
segments.s220-g64: بزیر سپر کرد is a verb phrase where بزیر (= under / beneath) + سپر (shield) form the place/goal and کرد is the light verb “made/did” used here to mean “put/placed” — i.e. “put (his head) under the shield.” سر ناپدید: سر = “head,” ناپدید = “vanished / became invisible.” The sequence expresses result: he put his head under the shield and disappeared.
segments.s220-g257: چنان دان is an imperative: دان (poetic/archaic imperative of ‘to know’) with چنان ‘thus/so’ = “know this/so”. که introduces a subordinate clause (here temporal/that-clause) giving the content: کیخسرو آمد بجنگ literally “Kay Khosrow came to fight” but functions as a present/future event in narrative—آمد (come) + بجنگ (ب + جنگ, infinitival/directional ‘to battle’) = “comes to battle/comes to fight.” Word order is typical epic Persian: main imperative + که + clause.
segments.s220-g258: مکن is the negative imperative ‘do not’. خیره دل is a noun phrase: خیره ‘astonished/dazed’ modifying دل ‘heart’; را is the definite/object marker attaching to the noun phrase. بدین is the prepositional/compound element meaning ‘by/with this’ (here “by this affair”), followed by کار ‘affair/deed’ and تنگ ‘narrow/tight’ used predicatively: “do not make the astonished heart narrow ( i.e. distressed) by this affair.” Word order: negative verb + object + prepositional cause + predicate.
segments.s220-g259: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘about/from’. The phrase has an inverted/exclamatory order: چه (interrogative/exclamative ‘what/how/why’) + رانی (address or epithet; here vocative/identifier) + تو ‘you’, followed by چندین سخن ‘many words’ — literally, “About Rostam, what (do you say), O Rani? many words.” In context the speaker reproves someone for speaking many words about Rostam; چندین modifies سخن.
segments.s220-g260: ز زابلستان ‘from/about Zabulistan’ (ز = from/about). یاد here is a verbal/noun form meaning ‘remembrance/recalling’; چندین ‘often/many a’ qualifies it; مکن is the negative imperative ‘do not’. The clause therefore commands: “Do not repeatedly recall/mention Zabulistan.” Word order: prepositional phrase + verbal noun + frequency modifier + negative imperative.
segments.s220-g261: This is a conditional clause introduced without an explicit ‘if’ particle but with مرا‌گر functioning as a conditional/ concessive element (‘if/should [he] see my…’). درفش 'standard/banner' is the subject/object; ببیند is the present/subjunctive form ‘he sees’ (here as protasis of the condition); به چنگ literally ‘into/into the claw/into his hand’ = ‘in (his) grasp’ with به as directional preposition and چنگ ‘claw/hand/grasp’ used figuratively for hold.
segments.s220-g262: بدریای چین بر is a compacted poetic phrase: بدریای (proper name or epithet ‘Badrīyā’/‘moonlike’) + چین ‘fold/wave’ with بر ‘upon/on’ marking position; خروشد is a present/future-form verb ‘will roar/bring forth a cry’ and نهنگ is the subject ‘whale’. Together: “a whale will uprear/roar upon the (Badrīyā) sea/folds.” The verb is in narrative/prophetic present (future sense in context).
segments.s220-g263: برو is the imperative ‘go’. لشکر آرای is a verb–object phrase in which آرای functions as an imperative/causative ‘array/ornament’ applied to لشکر ‘army’ (i.e. ‘array the army’). و is the coordinating ‘and’. برکش سپاه is another imperative sequence: برکش here is an archaic/poetic imperative ‘raise/lift up’ applied to سپاه ‘the host/army’ — together: “Go, array the army and raise the host.”
segments.s220-g264: درفش اندر آور is a command: درفش ‘banner’ + اندر آور ‘bring in/bring forth’ (آور here as imperative ‘bring’ with اندر ‘into/within’) = ‘bring the banner in/bring the standard’. به آوردگاه places the action ‘to the battlefield’: به = ‘to/towards’, آوردگاه = ‘battlefield/place of bringing’. Overall: “bring the banner into the battlefield.”
segments.s220-g105: پدید آمد is a compound past intransitive: پدید (appear) + آمد (came) = “appeared.” The demonstrative آن precedes the compound noun اژدهافش درفش (literally ‘dragon‑like banner’), so the verb is placed before its subject for poetic emphasis.
segments.s220-g106: شب (night) is the topical subject; تیره‌گون is an adjective used predicatively/adverbially (“dark‑hued/turned dark”) and کرد is the simple past causative/performative verb (“caused/made”). The clause states that (it) rendered the گیتی (world) بنفش (purple) — گیتی is the affected object and بنفش is its resulting description.
segments.s220-g107: چو introduces a temporal clause here (= “when”). The clause follows normal Persian SOV order: subject گودرز (Goudarz), object روی تهمتن (the face of Tahmatan), and the past verb بدید (saw) at the end.
segments.s220-g108: شد is the past of شدن (become) and takes the predicate ناپدید (vanished/unseen). از introduces the source/starting point (here roughly “from/out of”), and آب‌دیده is an epithet of رخش (Rakhsh) — ‘water‑seen/seasoned’ — placed before the steed’s name; the whole phrase means the steed became unseen/vanished from view.
segments.s220-g109: پیاده شد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘dismounted / became on foot.’ از اسپ is a prepositional phrase ‘from the horse.’ The conjunction و links this action to the following noun phrase رستم همان (Rostam himself/the very Rostam), with همان adding emphasis.
segments.s220-g110: پیاده بیامد is ‘came on foot’ (بیامد = came, past). چو introduces a simile here (= “as/like”), so چو باد دمان is a poetic comparison ‘like the wind at the hem,’ describing Rostam’s swift arrival.
segments.s220-g111: گرفتند is past plural ‘they seized/took.’ The object یکدگر را (‘one another’ with the object marker را) is the direct object; مر (‘me’) appears nearby to mark location/relationship (‘beside me’ or ‘at my side’), and کنار functions adverbially ‘beside/near.’ Word order reflects a topical placement of مر before the object.
segments.s220-g112: ز (from) + هر (each/every) here yields ‘from both’ (ablative), and برآمد is past ‘arose/issued forth.’ خروشی is the noun ‘a roar’ and بزار functions as an archaic/intensifying verb/adjective in this formula (here rendered ‘dreadful/terrible’ or ‘let out’), so the clause means ‘a (terrible) roar arose from the two.’
segments.s220-g345: بشد is an epic past/intransitive verb here (بَشُد) meaning ‘they went / it came to pass’. پیلتن is a place name (direct object/goal). تا marks the limit ‘as far as’ and governs the following locative phrase; سر تیغ کوه is a noun phrase ‘the head/crest (سر) of the mountain (تیغِ کوه)’, with سر (summit) modified by تیغِ کوه (the ridge/point of the mountain). Word order puts the verb before the place phrase in this compressed epic style.
segments.s220-g346: بدیدار is a contracted form of به دیدار ‘to the meeting / to meet’ functioning as a prepositional/infinitival phrase; it introduces the purpose or destination. خاقان و توران گروه are coordinated nouns: ‘the Khaghan and the Turanian band/host’ (خاقان = title, گروه = troop/band). The conjunction و links the two coordinated items.
segments.s220-g347: سپه دید is a simple past clause ‘the host/army saw’ or here functions to introduce magnitude (‘the host was so many’); چندانک (‘to that extent’) signals the degree/result clause that follows. دریای روم is the noun phrase ‘the Sea of Rûm/Byzantium’ (دریا = sea). Together the clause sets up a comparison of scale.
segments.s220-g348: از ایشان is an ablative/comparative adverbial ‘from them / compared with them’. نمودی is a past tense verb here meaning ‘appeared / showed’ with دریای روم as its subject. چو introduces the simile ‘like/as’, so چو یک مهره موم completes the comparison: the sea appeared like a single bead of wax when compared with that multitude.
segments.s220-g349: This segment is an enumeration of peoples/places using repeated و (‘and’). There is no verb; each item is an ethnonym or place-name in a list. The lexical items are proper names (some obscure), joined by و to form a long catalogue.
segments.s220-g350: Continuation of the list of peoples/regions, again coordinated by و. Each noun is a name (چغانی, رومی, وهری, سند); the whole phrase functions appositively as part of the catalogue of nations.
segments.s220-g351: جهانی شده is a nominal/predicative sequence ‘the world became’ (شده = perfect/passive participle used as past ‘became’). The three colors سرخ و زرد و سیاه are coordinated adjectives describing the changed appearance — literally ‘the world became red and yellow and black’ (i.e., varied colors everywhere).
segments.s220-g352: دگرگونه جوشن is a noun phrase ‘armor of a different kind’ (دگرگونه = different, modifying جوشن = cuirass/armor). دگرگون کلاه repeats the idea for helmets: دگرگون is a participial adjective ‘changed/transformed’ modifying کلاه ‘helmet/hat’. The two noun phrases summarize the visual change in equipment.
segments.s220-g193: گزافست is an older poetic form meaning “is capricious/absurd,” where -ست is the third‑person copula (shortened from است). کردار گردان سپهر is a noun phrase functioning as the subject/predicate complement: کردار “deeds” followed by the postpositive modifier گردان “revolving” and the head noun سپهر “heavens/sky.” Word order is typical older Persian nominal stacking (no overt izāfa).
segments.s220-g194: گهی … و گه … is a balanced antithetical construction meaning “sometimes … and sometimes ….” جنگست contains the copula -ست again: “(it) is war.” The second half omits the copula (elliptical) but is understood: “and sometimes (it is) delight and love.” نوش here is figurative “delight/pleasure,” and مهر “love/affection.”
segments.s220-g195: اگر introduces a subordinate conditional clause “if.” کشته‌گر is the agent noun “slayer/killer,” modified by مرده “dead.” هم here has the sense “even/also.” بگذریم is first‑person‑plural subjunctive/optative from گذشتن and in this context best read as “we let pass/forgive.” The whole clause = “if we even forgive a dead slayer.”
segments.s220-g196: سزدگر is used substantively as “that which is fitting/appropriate” (the thing proper to do). بچون و چرا is the idiomatic pair corresponding to “how (بچون ~ چون) and why (چرا)” — here asking about manner and reason. ننگریم is first‑person‑plural present/subjunctive of نگر(یدن) with negative prefix ن‑ that functions rhetorically; the line reads as a question/expectation: “should we not consider?” (i.e. “ought we not to look into how and why?”).
segments.s220-g197: چنان is an adverb “thus/so”; رفت (here) means “go/act” and is modified by the modal بایَد “one must/it is necessary.” که introduces the subordinate clause of time/purpose “that/when,” and آید زمان = “(the) time comes.” The construction expresses a required manner of acting relative to the coming time.
segments.s220-g198: مشو تیز is a negative imperative: مشو (do not become/be) + the adjective تیز “swift/hasty,” yielding “do not be hasty.” با + گردش آسمان is an instrumental/associative phrase “with the turning/circuit of the heavens,” i.e. act in accordance with the heavens’ rotation/fate. گردش is the noun “turning/rotation.”
segments.s220-g199: This is a two‑word titular phrase followed by the optative particle باد meaning “may.” جهاندار پیروزگر are epithets stacked before یار (“ally/friend”): “the world‑ruler, the victorious (one).” باد at the end is a poetic optative expressing a wish: “may (he) be (our) ally.”
segments.s220-g200: سر بخت دشمن is a genitive/attributive phrase meaning roughly “the fortune of the enemy” (سر here prefacing بخت as a poetic intensifier of fate/fortune). نگونسار is an adjective meaning “woeful/ill‑fated,” and باد again is the optative “may (it be).” The copula is omitted (typical in poetry), so the line reads as a wish: “may the enemy’s fortune be woeful.”
segments.s220-g289: چنین is an adverb ('thus/so') placed before the verb for a formal/poetic effect. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' and here occurs before the subject (inversion typical in poetry). کاموس جنگی is the speaker's name plus an epithet 'the warrior' (جنگی modifying کاموس). بمن is a clitic/prepositional phrase 'to me' marking the indirect object of گفت.
segments.s220-g290: که introduces the content of the reported speech ('that' / 'that you...') leading into an imperative. تو is the explicit subject 'you'. پیش‌رو is a predicative noun/adjective 'forerunner / vanguard' (here 'leader of the front'). باش is the present imperative of بودن 'be' addressing تو. زین انجمن is a prepositional/attributive phrase 'of this assembly/host' (زین is an older/poetic form equivalent to 'از این'/'به این' forming 'of/this'); it attaches to انجمن 'assembly/host'.
segments.s220-g291: بسی here intensifies the adjective (classical usage 'very, much'); سخت qualifies سوگندهای as 'severe/grievous'. سوگندهای is the plural possessive-marked noun 'oaths/vows' (object of the verb in the next segment). دراز further modifies سوگندهای as 'long' (long in duration/wording).
segments.s220-g292: بخورد here is the verb of the clause and participates in the fixed collocation سوگند خوردن 'to take/swear oaths' (past tense third-person). و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and/then'. بر is a prepositional particle that combines with the following verb to indicate motion 'up/on'. آهیخت is a past/telic verb meaning 'lifted/raised' (poetic form) and takes گرز 'mace' as its object. از فراز is an adverbial phrase 'from on high / aloft', specifying the upward direction of the lifting.
segments.s220-g293: که again introduces reported content ('that...'). امروز adverbially situates the declaration 'today/now'. من is the emphatic subject 'I'. جز means 'except/only' and restricts the action that follows. The sequence ب + دین is the fused classical prepositional phrase (به + این → 'by/with this') pointing to the instrument; گرز is the instrument 'mace' and جنگ is the abstract noun 'war/fighting' marking the domain of action (i.e. 'war by this mace').
segments.s220-g294: نسازم is a negative present-subjunctive/optative form 'I will not make/do' (here 'I will make no war' / 'I will not wage war' except as limited). وگر is a concessive conditional 'but if / and if even'. بارد functions here as a verb meaning 'should fall' (poetic present/subjunctive of باریدن 'to fall' used for rain/stones); it introduces the hypothetical concession. از ابر سنگ 'from the cloud stones' = 'stones falling from the clouds' (از = from, ابر = cloud, سنگ = stone). Together the phrase gives a strong concessive: 'even if stones fall from the clouds.'
segments.s220-g295: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (perfective third person). خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan' (ruler). بزد is the past tense verb 'struck/blew' and here governs کرنای ('to strike/blow the horn'); the verb+object order follows the narrative. کرنای is the object 'the trumpet/horn' (instrument producing the blast).
segments.s220-g296: تو is the subject 'you' in the following reported clause. گفتی here is the reporting verb 'you said' (past), introducing indirect speech with که. که marks the content clause 'that ...'. کوه is the theme 'the mountain'. اندر آمد is a compound-like verbal phrase 'came forth / moved' (اندر with آمد is a classical/poetic structure meaning 'came from/away'); ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form), and جای is 'place'. Together: 'you said that the mountain came from its place.'
segments.s220-g73: به پیش + سوار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward the front / forward' with سوار (horseman) as the core noun; اندر آمد is a verbal phrase (اندر + آمد) meaning 'came in / advanced' — a common poetic order where the manner/location phrase precedes the verb. دژم is an adjective placed after the verb phrase to describe the rider ('grim' or 'sullen'), a postpositive descriptive use typical of classical Persian.
segments.s220-g74: بزد is a simple past tense verb 'he struck / smote' with its object immediately following (تیغ = 'sword'), a compact poetic word order (normally با تیغ می‌زد). و شد links to a second clause ('and became'). نیزه او places the possessed noun before the pronoun (classical order: 'his spear'), and قلم is the predicate that follows شد; here قلم functions as a nominal predicate meaning something like 'rendered useless/broken' (poetic/archaic usage), so شد قلم = 'became [qalam].'
segments.s220-g75: ز قلب سپه is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart/core of the army' (ز = 'from', قلب = 'core/heart', سپه = 'army'). طوس is the subject, and چون introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when / as'; بنگرید (classical form with the b- prefix) is a past-tense/look verb 'he looked / gazed'. The clause order is typical: location phrase → subject → temporal marker → verb.
segments.s220-g76: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction where نگه (a noun 'a look / a gaze') combines with کرد ('did') to mean 'he cast a look' or 'he looked'. و links the clauses. جنگ دلیران is a genitive construction 'the battle/fighting of the brave ones' (جنگ = 'battle/fight', دلیران = plural 'brave ones'). بدید is the simple past 'he saw' and completes the sentence.
segments.s220-g77: بدانست is the past verb 'he knew / perceived'. Here کو functions as a subordinating/relative particle introducing the content of what was known ('that' / 'who'), an archaic usage in classical Persian. مرد کاموس نیست is the embedded clause 'the man Kamaus is not / does not exist' with نیست as the negative copula.
segments.s220-g78: چنو (چنان) here means 'such' and qualifies نیزه‌ور ('a spearman, spear‑bearer'); the phrase چنو نیزه‌ور = 'a spearman of that sort'. نیز is an adverb/conjunctive 'also/indeed' or an intensifier here, followed by جز طوس نیست 'there is none except Ṭūs' (جز = 'except', طوس = proper name, نیست = 'is not / there is none'). The whole clause states that no spearman of that calibre exists apart from Ṭūs.
segments.s220-g79: خروشان is an adverbial/manner word 'roaring' (present participial/adverbial use) describing the manner of coming. بیامد is the past 'came'. ز قلب سپاه is the source/location phrase 'from the heart of the army' (ز = 'from', قلب سپاه = 'the core of the host'). The sequence gives a vivid, compressed martial motion typical of epic diction.
segments.s220-g80: بیاری here means 'a companion / comrade' (subject). بر + گیو is a prepositional phrase 'against / upon Giv' (بر = 'against', گیو = proper name). شد + کینه‌خواه is a copular/resultative construction 'became an avenger' (شد = 'became', کینه‌خواه = 'avenger, one seeking vengeance'). Word order places the actor (بیاری) before the oblique phrase (بر گیو) and then the result 'شد کینه‌خواه'.
segments.s220-g273: وزان جایگه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'from that place' (وزان = 'from that / of that'); جایگه = 'place'. پیش خاقان چین is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the Khaghan of China' where پیش governs the title خاقان and چین specifies which khaghan ('China' as a geographic epithet). Together the segment locates the action: 'from that place, before the Khaghan of China'.
segments.s220-g274: بیامد and ببوسید are simple past narrative verbs in sequence: بیامد ('he came/approached') + ببوسید ('he kissed'). The following روی زمین is the object phrase: روی here is a noun 'surface/face' (the surface of the earth) governed by ببوسید, and زمین is the noun 'earth/ground'. The verbs are bare past forms used consecutively (action sequence).
segments.s220-g275: بدو گفت is an inverted dative + speech verb construction: بدو = 'to him / unto him' (dative clitic/phrase) and گفت is the verb 'said' (here introducing speech). شاها is a vocative 'O king!'. انوشه appears as a proper name (direct object/recipient). The final بدی here is a verb form 'give' addressed to the king (classical 2nd-person past/imperative-like form), so the short clause functions as an addressed command/speech: 'He said to him, “O King, give to Anoushe...”'.
segments.s220-g276: روانرا is روان + را (object-marked 'the soul' or 'the spirit' as definite object). بدیدار is a noun/verbal noun meaning 'a sight/meeting' or 'a last view' (literally 'a seeing/meeting'). توشه is an object meaning 'provision(s) / a travel‑pack'. The final بدی again is the 2nd‑person past/optative form of 'to give' used in the reported speech: 'give (to him) a seeing of the soul and give provisions.' Grammatically, روان را بدیدار توشه is a sequence of objects/nominal phrases governed implicitly by the verb بدی.
segments.s220-g277: بریدی is a past tense verb form (classical 2nd‑person past 'you cut/you took' or narrative past depending on context) and is followed by یکی راه ('one / a certain road') where یکی is indefinite ('a certain' / 'one'), راه = 'road/way'. دشوار و دور are coordinating adjectives 'difficult and distant' modifying راه. Word order is typical: verb + (indefinite) object + adjectives, with the adjectives following the noun.
segments.s220-g278: خریدی is another past verb form (literally 'you bought' in the lexical senses given, here used metaphorically 'you incurred/you brought upon'), followed by چنین ('such/thus') modifying رنج ('suffering/hardship'). ما را is the object 'us' with the object marker را, and بسور is a compound/phrase (ب + سور = 'to the feast / for the celebration') indicating the sphere or occasion affected. Grammatically the clause says that by that action the addressee 'bought/caused such suffering for us at the feast.'
segments.s220-g279: بدین سان is a fixed adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' (بدین = 'in this', سان = 'way/form'). بآزرم is 'با' + 'آزرم' meaning 'with reverence / with honour' (ba- prefix + noun/phrase of manner), and افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' as the object/target of the manner phrase. Together the segment sets the manner and respect accompanying the action: 'in this way, with reverence toward Afrasiab...'.
segments.s220-g280: گذشتی is a past tense verb 'you (or he) passed' (narrative past). به کشتی is a prepositional phrase 'by/with a ship' (به + کشتی). ز دریای آب is a poetic genitive/ablative 'from the sea of water' where ز = 'from' and دریای آب = 'the sea (of) water' (دریای is the ezāfe form 'sea of ...'). The segment describes the movement: 'passed by ship from the sea.'
segments.s220-g305: ز بس is a causal/prepositional phrase: ز (from/because of) + بس (here 'so many' / 'such a number of'). تخت پیروزه is a noun + adjective ('thrones of turquoise'); بر پشت پیل is the locative phrase 'on the elephant's back' with the preposition بر placed before پشت. Word order is somewhat inverted (cause first), so this whole segment supplies the cause/setting for what follows.
segments.s220-g306: This is a nominal/ adjectival phrase describing the result or appearance: درفشان = 'shining, glittering' (adjective), بکردار = 'in good form / well‑formed / of graceful appearance' (adjectival), دریای نیل = 'the Nile' (literally 'sea of the Nile'). The phrase effectively says that the Nile (metaphorical image) shone/appeared glittering; the verb 'became/was' is implied by the causal construction started in the previous segment.
segments.s220-g307: بچشم اندرون here functions like 'to/for the inner eye' (بچشم ≈ به چشم, 'to the eye/for the sight'); روشنایی نماند is a past negated predicate ('no/light did not remain' → 'no light remained'). Word order places the locative/affected phrase before the predicate, a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s220-g308: همی here is an adverbial intensifier ('any longer/still/indeed') and با روان 'with the soul' marks the experiencer; آشنایی نماند is again the past negative 'no familiarity/acquaintance remained.' The clause parallels the first (no light remained … no familiarity remained) and uses نماند as the main verb in both halves.
segments.s220-g309: پر از گرد شد is the light‑verb construction 'became full of dust' (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' + گرد 'dust' + شد 'became'). The logical subject follows the verb in poetic order: چشم و کام سپهر ('the eye and desire of the sky/heavens'). This inversion is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s220-g310: تو گفتی is a past statement 'you said' (to introduce what follows as reported). بقیر اندر اندود چهر is a noun phrase: بقیر 'with pitch/tar' + اندر 'in/within' + اندود (coating/smeared) modifying چهر 'face' — 'a face smeared with pitch.' Grammatically the second part is the reported clause/content of 'you said.'
segments.s220-g311: چو ... بیامد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when', بیامد = 'came/entered' (past). بقلب سپاه = 'into the heart/center of the army' uses ب- + قلب ('into the heart/center') as a locative phrase; سپاه is 'army/host.' Together: 'When the khagan came into the midst of the host...'.
segments.s220-g312: بچرخ here expresses revolving/turning (verbal/adverbial 'in the circling/revolution'); اندرون gives the internal location ('within'); ماه is the subject 'the moon'; گم کرد راه is the verbal phrase 'lost (its) way' — راه گم کردن = 'to lose one's way.' Grammatically, the locative modifier (بچرخ اندرون) frames where/when the moon 'lost its way.'
segments.s220-g337: بیاراست is a past/preterite verb meaning 'arrayed' or 'set in order' (here transitive with an implicit object 'his men'). The line places the verb before the subject (poetic inversion): گودرز is the subject 'Goudarz'. بر میمنه is an adverbial phrase 'on the right (side/flank)' with بر = 'on/upon'.
segments.s220-g338: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' or 'dispatched' (transitive). بر here marks direction 'to/toward' (he sent onto/to the mountain). کوه خارا is a noun phrase 'the flinty/rocky mountain' with خارا as an adjectival descriptor. بنه is used as a noun 'a small group/heap/party' (here 'a small party'), the direct object of فرستاد.
segments.s220-g339: This short clause lists names with a locative phrase: فریبرز and کاوس are proper names placed side by side; بر introduces the locative 'on' and میسره (here an adverbial/nominal phrase) marks the left side/position (poetic battlefield positioning). The clause is elliptical (no explicit verb) and functions as a location caption.
segments.s220-g340: جهان is the subject 'the world'. چون is the comparative/simile marker 'like' or 'as'. نیستان is a simile noun 'reed‑bed'; شده is the perfect/past participle 'has become' (here 'became'); یکسره is an adverb 'completely/wholly'. Together they form 'the world became wholly like a reed‑bed.'
segments.s220-g341: بقلب اندرون is a fused locative phrase 'in the inner heart / deep within'. طوس is the place/name in genitive/locative relation ('of Tus' or 'in Tus'). نوذر is the subject 'Nowzar'. بپای is a verbal phrase meaning 'stood firm/abided' (past sense 'held his ground'); the clause describes Nowzar's resolute position inside Tus.
segments.s220-g342: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'. شد is the past auxiliary 'became'. پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر = 'full', از = 'of/from'). ناله کرنای is a compound genitive: ناله 'lament/cry' qualified by کرنای 'of the horn/trumpet' (i.e. 'lamenting trumpet‑blasts').
segments.s220-g343: جهان is 'the world' and شد is 'became/was'. بگرد here is a verbal form meaning 'turned' (a poetic/archaic form of 'to turn' / 'to be turned'), and اندرون ناپدید describes the resulting state 'vanished/hidden within'. Together: 'the world turned inward and became unseen.'
segments.s220-g344: کسی ... ندید is a negative-result clause: کسی here is used as 'no one' (contextualized by the negative verb). از یلان = 'from/amid the heroes' (از as partitive 'among'). خویشتن را is the reflexive object 'his own (self/men)' with را the object marker. ندید is past negative 'did not see' (so: 'no one among the heroes saw his own').
segments.s220-g1: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); خورشید is the grammatical subject placed before the past tense verb زد (simple past of زدن) which here means 'to strike'. پنجه is the object-image ('claw') and is linked with the prepositional phrase بر پشت گاو ('upon the back of the ox') where بر governs the locative complement پشت گاو. The line uses an active past narrative order subject–verb–object with a poetic image.
segments.s220-g2: ز هامون is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain' placed at the start of the clause; برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. خروش چکاو is a nominal phrase in which خروش (the head noun) is specified by چکاو (the lark) in a genitive/possessive sense: 'the cry of the lark'. The verb follows the locative phrase, a common poetic word order.
segments.s220-g3: ز introduces the source ('from') and درگاه کاموس is a genitive construction 'the court/threshold of Kamus'. برخاست is the past-tense verb 'rose/rose up' and غو is the noun object/subject of that action (here 'uproar' — the construction is poetically inverted so the verb precedes the noun it affects).
segments.s220-g4: که begins an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/because'); او is the pronoun subject 'he'; بود is past tense 'was'. اسپ افگن is a noun followed by an adjectival epithet (اسپ 'horse/steed' modified by افگن 'shining/radiant'), and و links the coordinate predicate پیش رو where پیش 'before/in front' + رو ('toward/on') form the compound meaning 'in the forefront' or 'leading'. Overall the clause gives a cause/reason with past tense description.
segments.s220-g5: سپاه is the subject 'the army'; انجمن کرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'made an assembly' or 'assembled' (انجمن = assembly, کرد = did/held). The conjunction و links the second verb phrase; جوشن (cuirass/armor) is the direct object of the following verb بداد, which here functions as a past action 'gave'/'bestowed' (an older or dialectal past form).
segments.s220-g6: دلش is 'his heart' (possessive enclitic -ش). پر ز رزم is a predicative phrase 'full of battle' where پر 'full (of)' takes the complement with the preposition ز. The second clause mirrors the first in parallel structure: سرش 'his head' + پر ز باد 'full of wind' (باد = 'wind', used figuratively here). The conjunction و links the two parallel predicates.
segments.s220-g7: زره 'armor' is the subject-nominal and بود is the past copula 'was'. در زیر پیراهنش is a locative phrase 'under his tunic/shirt' (در زیر = 'under/beneath', پیراهنش = 'his tunic' with enclitic -ش). The clause states location/existence of the armor in past narrative tense.
segments.s220-g8: کله ترگ بود is a nominal clause where کله 'head' is predicated as ترگ 'a rag/shred' with بود 'was' (implying 'his head was a rag' or 'he had a rag on his head'). و links the next verbless nominal apposition: قبا جوشنش places two nouns side by side where قبا 'robe' is equated with جوشنش 'his cuirass' (جوشن + possessive -ش), i.e., 'his robe was his cuirass'. The second phrase omits an explicit verb, a common poetic device equating the two nouns.
segments.s220-g201: ازین پس (از + این + پس) is an adverbial phrase meaning "from now on / henceforth." همه کینه is the object phrase "all/ every grudge" placed before the verb. آوریم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative (classical/hortative) meaning "we shall / let us bring/renew;" باز ("again/back") modifies آوریم to yield "bring back / renew." Word order: adverbial front, object, then verb, with subject implied.
segments.s220-g202: جهان را uses را as the definite object marker marking "the world" as the thing affected. بایران is an adjectival/qualifying form from بایر ("barren/fallow") functioning as an object complement, so the verb takes the sense "cause/make X barren." نیاز آوریم is the same hortative 1pl construction as above, with نیاز ("need/privation") used in the compound نیاز آوردن "to bring need/privation / to make desolate."
segments.s220-g203: بزرگان همه is a noun phrase "the great ones, all of them" (noun followed by the quantifier همه). خواندند is the simple past 3pl of خواندن here meaning "they uttered/called out," so خواندند آفرین = "they cried/voiced 'āfarīn' (an acclamation). آفرین is used as an interjection "bravo / well done."
segments.s220-g204: که introduces a content clause expressing a wish: "that / (may it be) that..." بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase "without you." مبادا is a classical negative jussive/optative meaning "may it not be / let it not happen" and here governs an elided verb (e.g., باشد). زمان و زمین are the coordinated terms ('time and earth') that are the subjects/terms of the wish (i.e., 'may time and earth not be without you').
segments.s220-g205: همیشه is an adverb "always / ever" qualifying the following wishes. بدی نامبردار is an elliptical optative phrase: نام بردن = "to name/mention," so this construction functions as a negative wish in the larger clause (i.e., 'may evil not be named/mentioned'). و شاد coordinates a second wish 'and (may he be) joyful,' with the copula omitted (common in Persian poetry).
segments.s220-g206: در شاه پیروز is a locative/agent phrase 'in the victorious king' (or 'in the court of the victorious king'). بی‌تو مباد is the same negative optative as above ('may it not be without you'), with the main verb/copula omitted; together this supplies the negative wish for the preceding predicates (i.e., 'may there be no joy in the victorious king without you').
segments.s220-g207: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از کوه is the adverbial 'from the mountain.' بفروخت is a past form used poetically (derived from فروختن) here best read as 'parted / departed' or 'let go/left' rather than the literal 'sold'; it precedes the nominal epithet گیتی‌فروز ('world-illuminating') which functions as the subject. The verb–subject inversion is a poetic word order.
segments.s220-g208: دو زلف شب تیره is a noun phrase 'the two locks / tresses of the dark night' with تیره qualifying شب. بگرفت is the simple past 3sg meaning 'seized / took hold of,' and روز is the object 'the day' placed sentence-finally (SOV-like order typical in poetry). The image: night clutches or clings to the day with its dark tresses.
segments.s220-g321: 'چگونه' is an interrogative adverb 'how/what sort of'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was' (poetic past). 'بخشش آسمان' is a genitive noun phrase 'the bounty/gift of heaven' (word order is inverted for poetic effect: question word + copula + predicate).
segments.s220-g322: 'کرا' is an archaic interrogative/relative pronoun 'who / whom'; 'زین' here functions poetically as 'از این' = 'from these / among these' (this sense is not listed in the provided choices); 'بزرگان' is the plural 'great ones / nobles'; 'سرآید زمان' = 'when the time arrives' with 'سرآید' as a classical 3rd-person present/future 'arrives/comes' and 'زمان' = 'time'. The whole segment means 'when the time comes for one of these great men.'
segments.s220-g323: 'درنگی' is a noun 'a pause / hesitation'; 'نبودم' is past negated copula 'I was not' = 'I did not'; 'براه' (بِ راه) is a prepositional phrase 'on the road / en route'; 'اندکی' is a small-quantity adverb 'a little / even a little'. The order is poetic: negative past verb follows the noun, giving 'I did not pause on the road, even for a little.'
segments.s220-g324: 'دو منزل' = 'two stages' (منزل here = stage/stage-of-a-journey); 'همی کرد' is the classical past-habitual/perfective form with the particle 'همی' + past 'کرد', expressing repeated or customary past action ('would do' / 'used to do'); 'رخشم' in the narrative is the name of Rustam's horse ('Rakhsh') with the possessive suffix '-م' = 'my Rakhsh' (that specific proper-name sense is not among the listed senses); 'یکی' here means 'as one / in one go' or simply 'one' — so together: 'my Rakhsh would cover two stages in one.'
segments.s220-g325: 'کنون' = 'now / presently'; 'سم' = 'hoof' (part of the horse); 'این بارگی' — in context 'بارگی' refers to the load/burden or something carried (here 'this burden/thing attached to the horse'); 'کوفتست' is a classical perfective/passive-like form from 'کوفتن' meaning 'is struck / has been beaten/bruised'. The clause says the hoof (or related part) has been struck/bruised now.
segments.s220-g326: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' = 'from / because of'; 'راه دراز' = 'the long road'; 'اندر' = 'in/within' but here part of the periphrasis 'ز ... اندر آشوفتست' meaning 'has become (un)settled/disturbed because of'; 'آشوفتست' is a classical 3rd-person perfect/present 'is disordered/disturbed'. Thus the whole segment means 'because of the long road it has become distraught/disturbed.'
segments.s220-g327: 'نیارم' here functions as an address or reference 'my companion / my friend' (an archaic/vocative use of 'یار' with the suffix '-م'); 'برو' is a classical connective/adverbial 'thereupon / then' or can be read as 'went/then'; 'کرد' is the past verb 'did/made' (here 'mustered' or 'raised'); 'نیرو بسی' = 'much/ample force' (نیرو = force/troops; بسی = abundant/enough). Together: 'My companion then mustered ample force.'
segments.s220-g328: 'شدن' here marks the occurrence or onset 'there came to be / happened'; 'جنگ جویان' = 'war-seekers' i.e., fighters/warriors (جویان from 'جویدن' in the sense 'seekers'); 'به پیش' = 'toward/in front of / before'; 'کسی' = 'someone / some person' (indefinite). So the clause states that fighters came forward before someone (i.e., the warriors advanced to stand before a certain person/position).
segments.s220-g97: ازان = a poetic contracted form meaning “from that.” دیده گه is a compound (دیده + گه) meaning “the moment/place of seeing” or “the watchful moment”; the repetition of دیده is a poetic device (one instance as the noun “eye/seeing” and the second functioning as related to the moment). بگشاد is the past form (with the archaic prefix ب-) of گشادن “to open,” so بگشاد لب literally ‘opened (his) lip,’ an idiom for ‘opened his mouth/spoke.’ Word order is fronted: a temporal phrase (ازان ... گه) comes before the verb + object (بگشاد لب).
segments.s220-g98: که here introduces the result/clause (“for/that”), شد is the past of شدن ‘to become’ (so ‘became’). دشت is the subject ‘plain,’ پر is an adjective ‘full (of)’ which takes خاک ‘dust’ as its complement (دشت پر خاک = ‘the plain, full of dust’). و connects the coordinated predicate تاریک شب (‘dark night’): تاریک modifies شب. The clause gives the state that caused the mouth to open.
segments.s220-g99: پر از + noun is the common pattern ‘full of X’ where پر (predicate) precedes the thing that is full: پر از گفتگویست = ‘is full of conversation/talk.’ گفتگویست here is a poetic fusion of گفتگو + یست (است) meaning ‘(it) is talk/conversation.’ هامون و راغ follow as the nominative items described as full (they are either place-names or poetic nouns: هامون ‘the plain’ and راغ which can mean ‘longing’ or be a name).
segments.s220-g100: میان is a preposition meaning ‘among’ or ‘between’ and takes the following plural noun یلان ‘heroes.’ نیز is an adverb ‘also/too,’ and چندین modifies چراغ to give ‘several lamps.’ Note that Persian often leaves the verb (e.g. ‘there were’) implicit in poetic lines, so this reads ‘and among the heroes, too, several lamps (were seen).’
segments.s220-g101: همانا is an emphatic adverb ‘verily/behold.’ که here functions as the subordinating conjunction ‘when/that.’ آمد is the past ‘came.’ گو before a name is a poetic particle that may mark a name as ‘so-called/called’ or serve as an introductory particle; here it introduces the place/name پیلتن (Piltan). The whole small clause means ‘indeed, when (someone) came to Piltan’ (or ‘when Piltan came’ depending on construal), with the particle گو marking the following name.
segments.s220-g102: دمان is a noun meaning ‘the skirts/foothill’ or a place-name (here a group/region is meant) and is coordinated with the rest by و ‘and.’ ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ placed before زابل (Zabul, the region). یکی is an indefinite ‘one/a certain’ used here with انجمن ‘assembly/company’ to mean ‘one company’ or ‘a single assembled group.’ Word order: the preposition z + place precedes the quantified noun (‘one assembly’), producing the sense ‘and from Zabul came one company.’
segments.s220-g103: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ بشنید is the 3rd-person past of شنیدن ‘he heard.’ What follows are proper names in apposition (گودرز، کشواد، تفت) — in classical verse it is common to place a verb and then a list of names the verb concerns; here the names are the people who heard or whose coming was heard. The phrase should be read as ‘When Goudarz heard (it), (then) Keshvad and Taft ...’ (names are presented without additional particles).
segments.s220-g104: شب تیره = ‘the dark night’ (شب modified by adjective تیره). از is the preposition ‘from,’ and کوه خارا is ‘the flinty/rocky mountain’ (خارا here describing the mountain as hard or flinty, or serving as a place-name). برفت is the past form meaning ‘departed/went away/rose (up)’ (in older Persian poetic usage برفت functions as the past of رفت indicating movement away). The clause literally says that the dark night departed/rose from the flinty mountain (a poetic way to describe a change of light or scene).
segments.s220-g361: This subordinate clause begins with the conjunction که (introducing the clause). تا is here an adversative/temporal marker meaning 'when/until' (an archaic usage in poetry). چون 'when/how' combines with the verb نماید (present/subjunctive form of 'to show' used here as a simple present/future‑oriented verb) and the dative phrase بما 'to us' follows the verb, so the literal order is 'that until/when it shows to us'. چرخ مهر is a genitival phrase with چرخ 'heaven/wheel' modified by مهر 'favour/kindness' (i.e. 'the wheel/heavens of favour').
segments.s220-g362: This is the main interrogative clause: چه 'what' introduces بازی کند 'does/play' (کند is the present/subjunctive form of کردن used here as a simple present/future 'does/plays'). پیر گشته سپهر is a nominal phrase in which سپهر 'heavens/sky' is described by the perfect participle گشته 'having become' + پیر 'old' (i.e. 'the heavens that have grown old'). The clause asks what trick/play the (now) old heavens will perform.
segments.s220-g363: فرود آمد is a compound past tense verb meaning 'he descended/came down' (فرود = descent/alighting + آمد = came). از کوه is a straightforward prepositional phrase 'from the mountain'. The coordination و links the second clause دل بد نکرد, where دل 'heart' is the semantic subject/experiencer and بد کردن + نکرد (negated کردن in past) yields 'did not become disheartened / did not take it ill.'
segments.s220-g364: گذر بر سپاه is an elliptical verbal phrase meaning 'he passed by the army' (گذر used as 'passing/passage' + بر + سپاه 'the army'). The conjunction و links to سپهبد نکرد where سپهبد 'spahbed/commander' is the (implied) object/target and نکرد (negated کردن) means 'he did not do [ harm / attack / wrong ]' — the verb is elliptical in classical style, so the precise action is left implicit (context: he did not act against/spare the commander).
segments.s220-g365: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous past 'he kept saying' (همی marks continuity). The following تا introduces the reported clause (an archaic/classical use here equivalent to 'that' or simply introducing direct speech). من کمر بسته‌ام is a perfect construction—بسته‌ام 'I have bound' (i.e. 'I have girded my waist/loins') with من 'I' and کمر 'waist/girdle' as the internal components.
segments.s220-g366: بیک جای is an archaic contraction corresponding to به یک جای 'in one place' (بِـ + یک + جای). یک سال 'one year' is the duration phrase and ننشسته‌ام is the negative perfect/present‑perfect 'I have not sat / I have not been seated' (here idiomatically 'I have not stayed/settled'). Together the clause means 'I have not remained in one place for a year.'
segments.s220-g367: فراوان modifies سپه and functions adverbially/numerically 'many/plentiful'; سپه 'army' is the direct object of دیده‌ام (perfect 'I have seen'). پیش ازین is a temporal phrase meaning 'before this / previously' (پیش 'before' + archaic از + ین forming 'ازین'). Word order places the quantifier before the noun and the perfect verb after the object, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s220-g368: ندانم is the present/factual negative 'I do not know.' که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...' in which لشکر بود is a simple past clause 'there was an army' (لشکر 'army' as subject + بود 'was'). بیش ازین is a comparative phrase 'more than this' (بیش 'more' + ازین 'than this'), so the full sense is 'I do not know that there was an army greater than this.'

sentences.s220-s184: I have seen many armies before now; I do not know that there was an army greater than this.
